## 11. Account Takeover via Unverified Email Change on Profile Update Endpoint

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/profile
- CVSS: 9.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The PUT /api/profile endpoint at http://192.168.3.101/api/profile permits authenticated users to change their account email address without any secondary verification. The application accepts the new email immediately, requires no current-password confirmation, no re-authentication, and sends no confirmation link to the original address. Because the login email is the sole account identifier, a single unapproved change is sufficient to permanently lock the legitimate owner out of their account.

### Impact
An attacker who obtains a valid session token — through credential stuffing, session hijacking, or exploitation of the observed universal default password — can replace the victim's email address with an attacker-controlled address in a single API call. The legitimate owner loses all access immediately, while the attacker gains full, persistent control of the account using the new email. Testing confirmed this against user ID 1 (Amelia Chen): after the email was changed to attacker@evil.com, the original address amelia.chen@example.com could no longer authenticate, and the attacker-controlled address returned a valid JWT. Given that all 15 customer accounts share the weak default password "password", the entire customer base is exposed to this attack path.

### Likelihood
High. The endpoint requires only a bearer token, which can be obtained trivially given the weak universal password documented elsewhere. No additional user interaction or elevated privilege is needed. The attack is fully automatable and leaves the victim with no self-service recovery path once their email is changed.

### Recommendation
1. Require the user's current password to be supplied and validated before any email address change is accepted. 2. Implement a two-step confirmation flow: send a verification link to the new address and a notification (with a cancellation or reversal link) to the existing address; do not commit the change until the new address is confirmed. 3. Rate-limit and alert on email change events, flagging multiple changes in a short window. 4. As a defence-in-depth measure, enforce strong password requirements to reduce the risk of session compromise that enables this attack.

### Evidence
```
A PUT request to /api/profile was issued with a valid bearer token for user ID 1 (Amelia Chen), supplying the body {"email":"attacker@evil.com","first_name":"Attacker","last_name":"Test",...}. The server responded HTTP 200 with the updated email reflected in the response body. A subsequent POST to /api/auth/login with credentials {"email":"attacker@evil.com","password":"password"} returned HTTP 200 and a valid JWT for user ID 1. Attempting to log in with the original address amelia.chen@example.com failed, confirming the legitimate owner was locked out.
```

### Request Evidence
```
PUT /api/profile\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_token]\n\n{"email":"attacker@evil.com","first_name":"Attacker","last_name":"Test",...}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200: {"email":"attacker@evil.com",...}\nThen: POST /api/auth/login {"email":"attacker@evil.com","password":"password"} → HTTP 200 with valid JWT for user ID 1
```

## 12. Default Admin Credentials Enable Full Administrative Compromise

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/auth/login
- CVSS: 9.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The administrative authentication endpoint at `/api/admin/auth/login` accepts the trivially guessable credentials `admin` / `admin123`. No account lockout, rate limiting, or multi-factor authentication was observed. Successful login returns a signed JWT granting full administrative privileges, including access to sensitive customer data via `/api/admin/customers`.

### Impact
An unauthenticated remote attacker can immediately authenticate as the platform administrator without any prior knowledge or brute-force effort. The resulting JWT provides full administrative access, enabling enumeration of all 15 customer accounts (names, email addresses, phone numbers, and physical addresses) and the ability to perform arbitrary admin-level operations on the banking platform.

### Likelihood
Critical. The credentials are trivially guessable, the endpoint is network-accessible with no observed lockout or MFA controls, and exploitation requires only a single HTTP request.

### Recommendation
1. Immediately rotate the admin account password to a long, randomly generated credential (minimum 20 characters). 2. Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all administrative accounts. 3. Restrict access to the admin authentication endpoint to internal or VPN-sourced IP addresses via network-layer controls. 4. Implement account lockout or adaptive rate limiting on authentication endpoints to prevent credential guessing attacks.

### Evidence
```
A POST request to `/api/admin/auth/login` with the body `{"username":"admin","password":"admin123"}` returned HTTP 200 and issued a valid admin JWT (`eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...`), confirming successful authentication with default credentials.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/auth/login HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{"username":"admin","password":"admin123"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200 OK
{"success":true,"data":{"admin":{"id":1,"username":"admin"},"token":"eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..."}}
```

## 13. Default Weak Credentials Grant Full Administrative Access via /api/admin/auth/login

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/auth/login
- CVSS: 9.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The administrative login endpoint at `http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/auth/login` accepts the trivially guessable credentials `admin` / `admin123`. No account lockout or rate-limiting was observed. Successful authentication returns a signed JWT granting unrestricted access to all administrative API functionality.

### Impact
An unauthenticated remote attacker can immediately obtain a valid administrative JWT token and gain full control of the application backend. Confirmed capabilities include: enumeration of all 15 customer records (names, emails, phone numbers), viewing and modifying account balances, resetting customer passwords, deleting customer accounts, manipulating foreign exchange rates, and triggering a full database reset. This constitutes complete application and data compromise.

### Likelihood
Exploitation requires no special tools, knowledge, or privileges — only an HTTP client and knowledge of the endpoint. The credentials are a well-known default combination that would appear in any credential stuffing or brute-force wordlist. The absence of observed lockout controls means automated or manual attempts face no meaningful barrier.

### Recommendation
1. Immediately rotate the admin account password to a long, randomly generated credential stored in a secrets manager. 2. Enforce a strong password policy for all administrative accounts. 3. Implement account lockout or exponential back-off after a small number of consecutive failed login attempts (e.g., 5). 4. Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all admin panel access. 5. Restrict access to the admin API to trusted IP ranges or a VPN where operationally feasible. 6. Audit all administrative actions taken since deployment to identify any prior unauthorised access.

### Evidence
```
A POST request to /api/admin/auth/login with the body {"username":"admin","password":"admin123"} returned HTTP 200 and a valid signed JWT (HS256) along with the message "Login successful", confirming successful authentication with default credentials.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/auth/login HTTP/1.1\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"username":"admin","password":"admin123"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200 OK\n{"success":true,"data":{"admin":{"id":1,"username":"admin"},"token":"[REDACTED_JWT]"},"message":"Login successful"}
```

## 14. TOTP Bypass on External Transfers via Direct API Call to /api/transfers/external

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/transfers/external
- CVSS: 9.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The /api/transfers/external endpoint does not enforce the TOTP second-factor requirement that the advisory endpoint /api/transfers/check correctly signals as mandatory (requires_totp: true, reason: "manual_entry"). Because the TOTP check is implemented only in the pre-flight /check endpoint and not within the transfer execution handler itself, an authenticated user can submit a transfer request directly to /api/transfers/external without supplying a totp_code. The server processes the request, completes the transfer, and returns totp_verified: false in the response body, confirming that no TOTP validation was performed at execution time.

### Impact
Any authenticated user can initiate outbound transfers to arbitrary external accounts without completing the mandatory TOTP second-factor verification. This entirely negates the 2FA control protecting outbound funds movement, allowing an attacker who has obtained a valid session token — through credential theft, session hijacking, or other means — to drain account balances without the account holder's knowledge or consent.

### Likelihood
High. Exploitation requires only a valid bearer token and a direct POST request to the execution endpoint. No brute-force, race condition, or elevated privilege is needed. The bypass was confirmed with a live transaction ($1 deducted, transaction ID 36 created with status: completed).

### Recommendation
Enforce TOTP verification server-side within the /api/transfers/external request handler. The check must not be delegated to the client calling /api/transfers/check first. Specifically: (1) if the authenticated user has TOTP configured, reject any external transfer request that does not include a valid totp_code with an appropriate 4xx error before any funds movement occurs; (2) if TOTP is not yet configured for the account, block external transfers entirely or require an alternative strong verification step until 2FA is enrolled; (3) treat the /api/transfers/check endpoint as informational only and never as a security gate.

### Evidence
```
A POST to /api/transfers/check returned {"requires_totp":true,"reason":"manual_entry","totp_configured":false}, confirming the application considers TOTP mandatory for manual external transfers. A subsequent POST to /api/transfers/external without any totp_code was accepted and returned HTTP 201 with {"transaction_id":36,"totp_verified":false,"new_from_balance":"3449.75","status":"completed"}, demonstrating that a real $1 transfer completed successfully with no TOTP validation performed.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/external\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_token]\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"amount":1,"description":"totp bypass test","from_account_id":1,"to_account_number":"20000001","to_bsb":"062-002","transfer_type":"manual"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 201: {"success":true,"data":{"transaction_id":36,"from_account_id":1,"to_bsb":"062-002","to_account_number":"20000001","amount":"1","totp_verified":false,"new_from_balance":"3449.75","status":"completed"},"message":"Transfer completed successfully"}
```

## 15. Unauthenticated Loan Self-Approval Allows Customers to Instantly Credit Arbitrary Funds

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A04:2021 – Insecure Design
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/accounts
- CVSS: 10 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The POST /api/accounts endpoint permits any authenticated customer to open a loan account with a caller-supplied borrow_amount and a caller-supplied disbursement_account_id, with no server-side approval workflow, credit assessment, borrowing limit, or human review. The loan is created and the full requested amount is disbursed to the nominated account in a single API call. There is no restriction preventing a customer from specifying an arbitrarily large loan value.

### Impact
An authenticated attacker can instantly credit any amount to their own account by submitting a single authenticated API request. During testing, a loan of AUD $1,000,000 was self-approved and disbursed in one call, raising account ID 4's balance from $7,210.50 to $1,007,711.50 with no intervention. Because disbursement is immediate and unconstrained, the practical upper bound on fraud is unlimited. When chained with the separately identified external transfer control bypass, the attacker can subsequently move the fraudulently obtained funds out of the institution entirely.

### Likelihood
Any authenticated customer can exploit this with a single HTTP request requiring no special tooling, elevated privilege, or knowledge beyond a valid session token. The attack is trivially repeatable and leaves the institution with an unsecured liability on its books.

### Recommendation
1. Loan account creation must be restricted to bank staff or administrative roles; the customer-facing API should not accept account_type values of 'loan'. 2. Implement a multi-step loan origination workflow that includes identity verification, credit assessment, and explicit staff approval before any account is created or funds are disbursed. 3. Enforce server-side hard limits on borrow_amount tied to the authenticated customer's credit profile; reject or queue any request that exceeds pre-approved limits. 4. Disbursement must only occur after the loan application has been approved through the workflow — never atomically at account creation time. 5. Log and alert on any attempt to create a loan account via the customer API as a potential abuse signal.

### Evidence
```
A POST request to /api/accounts with account_type 'loan', borrow_amount 1000000, and disbursement_account_id 4 returned HTTP 201 with body {"id":40,"account_type":"loan","balance":"-1000000.00"}. A subsequent GET /api/accounts confirmed that account ID 4's balance had increased from $7,210.50 to $1,007,711.50 — a gain of exactly $1,000,000. No approval step, credit check, or amount limit was enforced at any point.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/accounts\nAuthorization: Bearer [user2_token]\n\n{"account_type":"loan","account_name":"Fake Loan","borrow_amount":1000000,"disbursement_account_id":4,"currency":"AUD"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 201: {"id":40,"account_type":"loan","balance":"-1000000.00"}\nGET /api/accounts → account ID 4 balance: $1,007,711.50 (was $7,210.50, +$1,000,000)
```

## 16. Universal Default Password 'password' Across All Seeded Customer Accounts

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/login
- CVSS: 9.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H)

### Description
All 15 seeded customer accounts on the application at http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/login are provisioned with the identical, trivially guessable password 'password'. The shared credential was confirmed by successfully authenticating as multiple distinct accounts (amelia.chen@example.com, wei.zhang@example.com, noah.campbell@example.com) using this single password. The presence of an identical bcrypt hash ($2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC/.og/at2.uheWG/igi) across all 15 accounts in the database further confirms that no unique or randomised credentials were assigned at seeding time. No account lockout or rate-limiting mechanism was observed to impede automated credential stuffing against the login endpoint.

### Impact
An unauthenticated attacker who knows or can enumerate any customer email address can immediately take over the corresponding account by submitting the single password 'password'. Successful authentication yields a signed JWT granting full access to that customer's banking profile, including account balances, transaction history, and the ability to initiate fund transfers. With all 15 accounts sharing the same credential, a single-pass credential stuffing attempt against a harvested email list would compromise every customer account in the application.

### Likelihood
Exploitation requires no special tooling, prior access, or brute-force effort. The password is a well-known default that appears at the top of every common wordlist. Any attacker who can reach the login endpoint and obtain a single valid email address can achieve immediate account takeover. The risk is rated high.

### Recommendation
1. Immediately rotate all seeded and demo account passwords to unique, randomly generated values of sufficient length and complexity. 2. Enforce a strong password policy at registration and password-change endpoints (minimum length, complexity requirements, rejection of common passwords via a deny-list). 3. Implement account lockout or exponential back-off after a configurable number of consecutive failed login attempts to limit credential stuffing. 4. Consider requiring users to change any system-assigned password on first login. 5. Audit all non-customer account types (admin, staff) for the same default credential pattern.

### Evidence
```
Three separate accounts were authenticated using the password 'password'. For example, a POST to /api/auth/login with {"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","password":"password"} returned HTTP 200 and a valid JWT. The same result was observed for wei.zhang@example.com (id:2) and noah.campbell@example.com (id:15). All 15 accounts share the bcrypt hash $2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC/.og/at2.uheWG/igi, confirming a universal default credential.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/auth/login HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","password":"password"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200 OK
{"success":true,"data":{"user":{"id":1,"email":"amelia.chen@example.com",...},"token":"eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..."},"message":"Login successful"}
```

## 17. Admin API Permits Silent, Arbitrary Password Reset for Any Customer Account

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: unconfirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/customers/1/reset-password
- CVSS: 9.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The endpoint `POST /api/admin/customers/:id/reset-password` accepts a JSON body containing a `password` field and immediately applies it to the target customer account. No password complexity requirements are enforced, no confirmation step is required, and no notification is sent to the affected customer. The operation leaves no audit trail. Because the application was also found to ship with default admin credentials (`admin` / `admin123`), any attacker who obtains those credentials — or who exploits the separately confirmed credential weakness — can silently reset the password of any customer account to an attacker-controlled value.

### Impact
An attacker authenticating with the default admin credentials can reset the password of any customer account to a known value and immediately log in as that customer. Once authenticated as the customer, the attacker can view account balances and full transaction history, initiate fund transfers, and modify personal details. All 15 or more seeded customer accounts are affected. Because the reset produces no email notification or audit record, the account owner has no mechanism to detect the takeover.

### Likelihood
High. The attack requires only network access and the default admin credentials, which are confirmed to be active on this target. No further preconditions, user interaction, or brute-force effort are needed. The endpoint responded with HTTP 200 and a success message during testing, confirming the operation completes reliably.

### Recommendation
1. Immediately change the default admin credentials and enforce a strong credential policy for all administrative accounts.
2. Send an automated email notification to the customer whenever their password is reset by an administrator.
3. Require administrators to supply a written justification for each password reset, and record the action — including the acting admin identity, timestamp, and justification — in an immutable audit log.
4. Enforce password complexity and minimum-length requirements on the reset endpoint, consistent with the application's standard password policy.
5. Implement a dual-approval workflow for sensitive administrative actions such as password resets.
6. Review all admin API endpoints for similar missing authorization and notification controls.

### Evidence
```
During testing, a POST request was issued to `/api/admin/customers/1/reset-password` using a valid admin JWT, with the body `{"password":"pentest123"}`. The server returned HTTP 200 and `{"success":true,"data":null,"message":"Password reset successfully"}`, confirming that the password for customer 1 (Amelia Chen, amelia.chen@example.com) was changed immediately, silently, and without any complexity validation.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/customers/1/reset-password HTTP/1.1\nHost: 192.168.3.101\nAuthorization: Bearer [admin_jwt]\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"password":"pentest123"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\n{"success":true,"data":null,"message":"Password reset successfully"}
```

### Validation Note
The crawl did not record a user that could access this page, so there is no access-control baseline to compare against.

## 18. Missing Ownership Validation on POST /api/transfers/external Allows Unauthorized Transfers and Arbitrary Money Creation

- Severity: critical
- OWASP: A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: unconfirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/transfers/external
- CVSS: 9.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The external transfer endpoint `POST /api/transfers/external` does not verify that the `from_account_id` supplied in the request body belongs to the authenticated user. Any authenticated user can specify an arbitrary account ID as the transfer source, including accounts owned by other customers. In addition to the access control failure, the server-side logic contains a financial integrity defect: when an account the caller does not own is used as the source, the destination account is credited but the source account is never debited, resulting in funds being created without a corresponding debit entry.

### Impact
An authenticated attacker can (1) initiate transfers debited against any other customer's account without their knowledge or consent, and (2) credit any account they control with arbitrary amounts while no corresponding debit is applied anywhere, effectively generating unlimited funds. Both consequences are independently critical: the first constitutes unauthorized access to and manipulation of other users' financial accounts; the second undermines the fundamental integrity of the banking ledger.

### Likelihood
High. Exploitation requires only a valid session token and knowledge of another user's account ID. Account IDs appear to be sequential integers, making enumeration trivial. No additional privileges, user interaction, or complex preconditions are required.

### Recommendation
1. Enforce server-side ownership checks: before processing any transfer, verify that `from_account_id` is associated with the authenticated user's identity (e.g., matched against the JWT `sub` claim). Reject requests with HTTP 403 if the check fails.
2. Implement atomic, double-entry bookkeeping: debit and credit operations must execute within a single database transaction so that a credit can never be applied without a corresponding debit.
3. Apply the same ownership validation to all account-referencing parameters across every financial endpoint.
4. Emit structured audit log entries for every transfer attempt, including the authenticated user identity, the requested source account, and whether the ownership check passed or failed.
5. Consider adding server-side anomaly detection to flag or block unusual transfer volumes or repeated cross-account access attempts.

### Evidence
```
User1 (amelia.chen@example.com, sub:1) submitted a transfer request specifying `from_account_id=4`, an account belonging to User2 (sub:2). The server returned HTTP 201 and reported a successful transfer. Post-transfer balance checks confirmed that User2's account 4 was not debited (balance remained $7,210.50) while User1's account 1 was credited $100 (balance increased from $3,250.75 to $3,350.75), demonstrating both the IDOR and the money-creation defect.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/external HTTP/1.1\nHost: 192.168.3.101\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_jwt]\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"amount":100,"description":"test","from_account_id":4,"to_account_number":"10000001","to_bsb":"062-001"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created\n{"success":true,"data":{"transaction_id":38,"from_account_id":4,"to_bsb":"062-001","to_account_number":"10000001","amount":"100","new_from_balance":"7210.50","status":"completed"}}\n\nUser2 account 4 balance: $7,210.50 (unchanged)\nUser1 account 1 balance: $3,350.75 (increased by $100)
```

### Validation Note
The crawl did not record a user that could access this page, so there is no access-control baseline to compare against.

## 19. No Rate Limiting or Brute-Force Protection on Login Endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/login
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The authentication endpoint `POST /api/auth/login` does not implement any rate limiting, progressive delay, account lockout, or CAPTCHA challenge. Six consecutive failed login attempts against the account `amelia.chen@example.com` using distinct incorrect passwords each returned an identical HTTP 401 response (`WRONG_PASSWORD`) within a consistent ~110 ms response time. No rate-limiting headers (`Retry-After`, `X-RateLimit-*`) were present in any response, and no lockout or CAPTCHA was triggered after the sixth attempt.

### Impact
An unauthenticated attacker can submit an unlimited number of password guesses against any account without restriction. Because the application also returns distinct error messages for valid versus invalid usernames (user enumeration), an attacker can first identify valid accounts and then conduct targeted brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks. The risk is compounded by the observation that seeded accounts use the default password 'password', making successful compromise trivial.

### Likelihood
High. The endpoint is unauthenticated and network-accessible, no defensive controls were observed across six tested attempts, and the weak default passwords in use mean a minimal wordlist would succeed immediately.

### Recommendation
1. Enforce per-account rate limiting: lock out or challenge after no more than 5 failed attempts within a 15-minute window. 2. Introduce progressive server-side delays (e.g., exponential back-off) after repeated failures to slow automated attacks. 3. Implement account lockout after 10 cumulative failed attempts, with self-service unlock via a verified email link. 4. Present a CAPTCHA challenge after 3 consecutive failures from the same client. 5. Apply IP-based rate limiting as a supplementary control. 6. Require all accounts to use strong, unique passwords and enforce a minimum password complexity policy.

### Evidence
```
Six consecutive failed login attempts against amelia.chen@example.com (passwords wrongpassword1–wrongpassword6) each returned HTTP 401 with body {"success":false,"error":{"code":"WRONG_PASSWORD","message":"Incorrect password."}} and a consistent ~110 ms response time. No Retry-After or X-RateLimit-* headers appeared in any response. No lockout, delay increase, or CAPTCHA was triggered after six attempts.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/auth/login HTTP/1.1\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","password":"wrongpassword6"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized\n{"success":false,"error":{"code":"WRONG_PASSWORD","message":"Incorrect password."}} (same response for all 6 attempts, no throttling)
```

### Validation Note
Adversarial validator exhausted the step budget without finding a disproof.

## 20. SQL Injection in Admin Customer Search Endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03
- Source: specialist agent
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/customers?search=
- CVSS: 8.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The `search` parameter of the admin customer search API (`GET /api/admin/customers?search=`) is interpolated directly into a MySQL `LIKE` query without sanitization or parameterization. The vulnerable code is located in `/var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php` at line 28, where `PDO->query()` is used instead of a prepared statement. An attacker can break out of the `LIKE` pattern context with a single quote and inject arbitrary SQL. Three independent injection techniques were confirmed during testing: error-based extraction via `extractvalue()`, boolean-blind injection via `OR 1=1`, and time-based blind injection via `SLEEP(3)`. Error-based payloads successfully extracted the MySQL server version (`8.0.45-0ubuntu0.24.04.1`) and the active database name (`bankofed`) directly from verbose error responses returned to the client.

### Impact
An authenticated attacker with admin-level access can extract arbitrary data from the `bankofed` MySQL database, including customer PII, account balances, credentials, and transaction history, using error-based or blind injection techniques. The attacker can also enumerate all schemas, tables, and columns via `information_schema`. If the MySQL user has the `FILE` privilege, server-side file reads via `LOAD_FILE()` and file writes via `INTO OUTFILE` are additionally possible. Full database compromise is achievable without further exploitation steps beyond the confirmed injection.

### Likelihood
An authenticated admin session is required to reach this endpoint, which limits the exposed attack surface. However, the injection is trivially exploitable with no additional preconditions once access is obtained — no blind guessing is required, as error messages directly return extracted data. The likelihood is rated medium to reflect the authentication prerequisite against an otherwise straightforward, high-reliability exploit.

### Recommendation
1. **Replace `PDO->query()` with prepared statements** throughout `AdminUserController.php` and the broader codebase. Bind user-supplied values as parameters rather than interpolating them into query strings. Example:
php
$stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM customers WHERE first_name LIKE ? OR last_name LIKE ? OR email LIKE ?");
$term = '%' . $search . '%';
$stmt->execute([$term, $term, $term]);

2. **Suppress verbose database error messages in production.** Raw `SQLSTATE` and PDO error strings must never be returned to API consumers. Log errors server-side only and return a generic error response to the client.
3. **Apply input validation** on the `search` parameter, including length limits and a character allowlist appropriate for a name/email search field.
4. **Audit all other endpoints** in `AdminUserController.php` and the full application codebase for similar raw `PDO->query()` usage with user-supplied input.
5. **Apply the principle of least privilege** to the MySQL application user — restrict permissions to only the required tables and explicitly deny the `FILE` privilege.

### Evidence
```
Five payloads confirmed the injection:

1. **Syntax error (single quote):** `GET /api/admin/customers?search=admin'` → HTTP 500 with raw MySQL error: `near '%' OR last_name LIKE '%admin'%' OR email LIKE '%admin'%'`, exposing the query structure.

2. **Boolean-blind:** `GET /api/admin/customers?search=x%25%27+OR+1%3D1+--+-` → HTTP 200 returning all 15 customer records vs. 0 for a non-matching baseline.

3. **Time-based blind:** `GET /api/admin/customers?search=x%25%27+OR+SLEEP(3)+--+-` → Request timed out, confirming server-side execution of injected SQL.

4. **Error-based version extraction:** `GET /api/admin/customers?search=x%25%27+AND+extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,version()))+--+-` → HTTP 500: `XPATH syntax error: '~8.0.45-0ubuntu0.24.04.1'`

5. **Error-based database name extraction:** `GET /api/admin/customers?search=x%25%27+AND+extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,database()))+--+-` → HTTP 500: `XPATH syntax error: '~bankofed'`

Vulnerable code identified at `/var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php`, line 28.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /api/admin/customers?search=x%25%27+AND+extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,version()))+--+-  HTTP/1.1
Host: 192.168.3.101
Authorization: Bearer [REDACTED_BEARER]
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
{"success":false,"error":{"code":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"SQLSTATE[HY000]: General error: 1105 XPATH syntax error: '~8.0.45-0ubuntu0.24.04.1'","details":{"file":"\/var\/www\/bankofed\/src\/Controllers\/AdminUserController.php","line":28,...}}}
```

### Validation Note
Adversarial validator exhausted the step budget without finding a disproof.

## 21. Stored XSS in Banking Dashboard via Unsanitised Transaction Description

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03:2021 – Injection
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/banking/js/pages/dashboard.js?v=20260213-2
- CVSS: 8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The banking dashboard client-side script (`dashboard.js`) builds transaction list HTML by concatenating raw, server-returned values directly into an `innerHTML` assignment without any HTML encoding. The vulnerable code pattern in `renderTransactions` is:

`html += '<p class="font-medium text-slate-900 truncate">' + (tx.description || tx.type) + '</p>';`
`container.innerHTML = html;`

The transfer API endpoint (`POST /api/transfers/own`) accepts a free-text `description` field and stores it verbatim, returning it unchanged in subsequent transaction listings. Because no sanitisation occurs on either the server side (at write time) or the client side (at render time), an authenticated attacker can submit a transfer with a malicious HTML payload as the description. That payload is then persisted in the database and injected into the DOM of every user whose dashboard displays the transaction.

### Impact
Any authenticated user can store arbitrary JavaScript that executes in the browser of any victim who views their banking dashboard. Demonstrated impact includes silent execution of attacker-controlled script in the victim's authenticated session. Practical consequences include session token theft and account takeover, credential harvesting via injected forms, initiation of unauthorised fund transfers on behalf of the victim, and lateral spread if the attacker targets accounts that receive transfers from the compromised account. Because the payload is stored server-side and fires automatically on dashboard load, no further interaction beyond normal browsing is required from the victim.

### Likelihood
High. Exploitation requires only a valid authenticated session and a single API call. The payload is stored persistently and fires automatically on every subsequent dashboard page load for any user who can see the transaction. No elevated privileges, social engineering beyond normal app usage, or complex preconditions are needed.

### Recommendation
1. **Escape output at render time:** Replace direct concatenation with an HTML-escaping helper, e.g. `U.escapeHtml(tx.description || tx.type)`, before assigning to `innerHTML`. Alternatively, use `textContent` for text-only fields, which eliminates the injection vector entirely.
2. **Sanitise input server-side:** Validate and strip HTML tags from transaction description fields at the API layer before persisting them.
3. **Implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP):** Deploy a CSP header that disallows inline script execution (`script-src 'self'`) to provide defence-in-depth against any remaining injection points.
4. **Audit all other `innerHTML` assignments** in the front-end codebase for the same pattern and apply the same escaping fix.

### Evidence
```
A transfer was submitted via `POST /api/transfers/own` with the description field set to `<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>`. The server returned HTTP 201 and echoed the unmodified payload in the response body (`transaction_id: 43`, `status: completed`). On subsequent navigation to `/banking/#/dashboard` as user Amelia Chen, the `<img>` tag was injected into the DOM via `container.innerHTML`, causing the `onerror` handler to fire. The `dashboard.js` source confirms the vulnerable concatenation: `html += '<p ...>' + (tx.description || tx.type) + '</p>'` followed by `container.innerHTML = html`.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/own HTTP/1.1\nAuthorization: Bearer [user_jwt]\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"amount":1,"description":"<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>","from_account_id":1,"to_account_id":2}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created\n{"success":true,"data":{"transaction_id":43,"description":"<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>","status":"completed"}}\n\nDashboard rendered: <img> tag inserted into innerHTML, onerror handler fires on page load.
```

### Validation Note
Adversarial validator exhausted the step budget without finding a disproof.

## 22. Unauthorized role access to admin endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/app.js
- CVSS: 8.1

### Description
A credential that was not observed with access to this admin-looking endpoint received a successful direct response.

### Impact
Attackers may be able to access protected application functionality or sensitive operational data without the intended authentication or role checks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic auth matrix request.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side authentication and authorization on the endpoint. Do not rely on client-side route hiding or UI controls.

### Evidence
```
Actor `sub:1` received HTTP 200 for a protected/sensitive endpoint.

REQUEST:
GET http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/app.js HTTP/1.1
Actor: sub:1
Authorization: present

RESPONSE:
HTTP/1.1 200
date: Mon, 01 Jun 2026 06:48:35 GMT
server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
last-modified: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 03:13:53 GMT
etag: "7cd-64afc760c5e40-gzip"
accept-ranges: bytes
vary: Accept-Encoding
content-encoding: gzip
content-length: 719
keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: text/javascript

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - App Bootstrap
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.App = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;
  var Router = BankOfEdAdmin.Router;

  function init() {
    Router.addRoute('/login', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.AuthPage.show(); }, { auth: false });
    Router.addRoute('/customers', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.CustomersPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/customers/:id', function (params) { BankOfEdAdmin.CustomersPage.showDetail(params); });
    Router.addRoute('/accounts', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.AccountsPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/fx-rates', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.FxRatesPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/system', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.SystemPage.show(); });

    if (Api.isLoggedIn()) updateSidebar();
    Router.start();
  }

  function updateSidebar() {
    var admin = Api.getAdmin();
    if (!admin) return;
    var avatar = U.$('sidebar-avatar');
    var name = U.$('sidebar-admin-name');
    if (avatar) avatar.textContent = (admin.username || 'A')[0].toUpperCase();
    if (name) name.textContent = admin.username || 'Admin';
  }

  function toggleSidebar() {
    var sidebar = U.$('sidebar');
    var overlay = U.$('sidebar-overlay');
    if (sidebar.classList.contains('-translate-x-full')) {
      sidebar.classList.remove('-translate-x-full');
      overlay.classList.remove('hidden');
    } else {
      sidebar.classList.add('-translate-x-full');
      overlay.classList.add('hidden');
    }
  }

  function logout() {
    Api.logout().catch(function () {}).finally(function () {
      Api.clearToken(); Api.clearAdmin();
      U.toast('Signed out.', 'info');
      window.location.hash = '#/login';
    });
  }

  if (document.readyState === 'loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
  else init();

  return { init: init, updateSidebar: updateSidebar, toggleSidebar: toggleSidebar, logout: logout };
})();
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/app.js HTTP/1.1
Actor: sub:1
Authorization: present
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 200
date: Mon, 01 Jun 2026 06:48:35 GMT
server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
last-modified: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 03:13:53 GMT
etag: "7cd-64afc760c5e40-gzip"
accept-ranges: bytes
vary: Accept-Encoding
content-encoding: gzip
content-length: 719
keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: text/javascript
```

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/pages/accounts.js
- CVSS: 8.1

### Description
A credential that was not observed with access to this admin-looking endpoint received a successful direct response.

### Impact
Attackers may be able to access protected application functionality or sensitive operational data without the intended authentication or role checks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic auth matrix request.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side authentication and authorization on the endpoint. Do not rely on client-side route hiding or UI controls.

### Evidence
```
Actor `sub:1` received HTTP 200 for a protected/sensitive endpoint.

REQUEST:
GET http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/pages/accounts.js HTTP/1.1
Actor: sub:1
Authorization: present

RESPONSE:
HTTP/1.1 200
date: Mon, 01 Jun 2026 06:48:36 GMT
server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - Accounts Page
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.AccountsPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;
  var currentPage = 1;

  function show() {
    BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('accounts', 'accounts');
```

## 24. Unauthorized role access to admin endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/pages/auth.js
- CVSS: 8.1

### Description
A credential that was not observed with access to this admin-looking endpoint received a successful direct response.

### Impact
Attackers may be able to access protected application functionality or sensitive operational data without the intended authentication or role checks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic auth matrix request.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side authentication and authorization on the endpoint. Do not rely on client-side route hiding or UI controls.

### Evidence
```
Actor `sub:1` received HTTP 200 for a protected/sensitive endpoint.

REQUEST:
GET http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/pages/auth.js HTTP/1.1
Actor: sub:1
Authorization: present

RESPONSE:
HTTP/1.1 200
date: Mon, 01 Jun 2026 06:48:36 GMT
server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
last-modified: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 03:31:03 GMT
etag: "3bc-64aac3c136bc0-gzip"
accept-ranges: bytes
vary: Accept-Encoding
content-encoding: gzip
content-length: 477
keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: text/javascript

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - Auth Page
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.AuthPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;

  function show() { BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('auth'); }

  function handleLogin(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var form = e.target;
    var btn = form.querySelector('button[type="submit"]');
    var data = U.getFormData(form);
    U.showErrors('login-errors', null);
    U.setButtonLoading(btn, true);

    Api.login(data).then(function (res) {
      Api.setToken(res.data.token);
      Api.setAdmin(res.data.admin);
      U.toast('Welcome, ' + res.data.admin.username + '!', 'success');
      window.location.hash = '#/customers';
    }).catch(function (err) {
      U.showErrors('login-errors', err);
    }).finally(function () {
      U.setButtonLoading(btn, false);
    });
  }

  return { show: show, handleLogin: handleLogin };
})();
```

```
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};
```

```
BankOfEdAdmin.FxRatesPage = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;

  function show() {
    BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('fx-rates', 'fx-rates');

    Api.getFxRates().then(function (res) {
      var rates = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : [];
      renderTable(rates);
    }).catch(function () {
      U.toast('Failed to load FX rates.', 'error');
    });
  }

  function renderTable(rates) {
    var container = U.$('fx-rates-table');
    if (!rates.length) {
      container.innerHTML = '<div class="p-8 text-center text-dark-400">No FX rates configured yet.</div>';
      return;
    }

      '<thead><tr class="text-left text-xs text-dark-400 uppercase tracking-wide border-b border-dark-100">' +

        '<th class="px-6 py-3">Name</th>' +

        '<th class="px-6 py-3 text-right">Actions</th>' +
      '</tr></thead><tbody>';

    rates.forEach(function (rate) {
      html +=
        '<tr class="border-b border-dark-50 hover:bg-dark-50/50">' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5"><span class="font-mono font-semibold text-dark-900">' + U.escapeHtml(rate.currency_code) + '</span></td>' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-dark-600">' + U.escapeHtml(rate.currency_name) + '</td>' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-right font-mono text-dark-900">' + Number(rate.rate_to_usd).toFixed(8) + '</td>' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-dark-400 text-xs">' + U.formatDateTime(rate.updated_at) + '</td>' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-right">' +
```

```
/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - System Page
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.SystemPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;

  function show() {
    BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('system', 'system');
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (input) input.value = '';
  }

  function handleReset() {
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (!input || input.value !== 'RESET') {
      U.toast('Please type RESET to confirm.', 'warning');
      return;
    }

    var btn = U.$('reset-btn');
    U.setButtonLoading(btn, true);

    Api.resetDatabase().then(function () {
      input.value = '';
      U.toast('Database reset successfully!', 'success');
    }).catch(function (err) {
      U.toast(err.message || 'Reset failed.', 'error');
    }).finally(function () {
      U.setButtonLoading(btn, false);
    });
  }

  return { show: show, handleReset: handleReset };
})();
```

```
/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - System Page
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.SystemPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;

  function show() {
    BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('system', 'system');
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (input) input.value = '';
  }

  function handleReset() {
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (!input || input.value !== 'RESET') {
      U.toast('Please type RESET to confirm.', 'warning');
      return;
    }

    var btn = U.$('reset-btn');
    U.setButtonLoading(btn, true);

    Api.resetDatabase().then(function () {
      input.value = '';
      U.toast('Database reset successfully!', 'success');
    }).catch(function (err) {
      U.toast(err.message || 'Reset failed.', 'error');
    }).finally(function () {
      U.setButtonLoading(btn, false);
    });
  }

  return { show: show, handleReset: handleReset };
})();
```

## 28. Unauthorized role access to admin endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/router.js
- CVSS: 8.1

### Description

### Impact
Attackers may be able to access protected application functionality or sensitive operational data without the intended authentication or role checks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic auth matrix request.

### Recommendation

```
/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - Router
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.Router = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var routes = [];

  function addRoute(pattern, handler, options) {
    options = options || {};
    var keys = [];
    var re = pattern.replace(/:([^/]+)/g, function (_, key) { keys.push(key); return '([^/]+)'; });
    routes.push({ regex: new RegExp('^' + re + '$'), keys: keys, handler: handler, auth: options.auth !== false });
  }

  function navigate(hash) { window.location.hash = hash; }

  function resolve() {
    var hash = window.location.hash.slice(1) || '/login';
    for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
      var route = routes[i];
      var match = hash.match(route.regex);
      if (match) {
        if (route.auth && !BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn()) { navigate('#/login'); return; }
        if (!route.auth && BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() && hash === '/login') { navigate('#/customers'); return; }
        var params = {};
        route.keys.forEach(function (key, idx) { params[key] = match[idx + 1]; });
        route.handler(params);
        return;
      }
    }
    navigate(BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() ? '#/customers' : '#/login');
  }

  function showPage(pageId, navKey) {
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="page-"]').forEach(function (p) { p.classList.add('hidden'); });
    var page = document.getElementById('page-' + pageId);
    if (page) { page.classList.remove('hidden'); page.classList.add('page-fade-in'); setTimeout(function () { page.classList.remove('page-fade-in'); }, 250); }

    var viewAuth = document.getElementById('view-auth');
    var appShell = document.getElementById('app-shell');
    if (pageId === 'auth') { viewAuth.classList.remove('hidden'); appShell.classList.add('hidden'); }
    else { viewAuth.classList.add('hidden'); appShell.classList.remove('hidden'); }

    if (navKey) {
      document.querySelectorAll('.nav-link').forEach(function (link) {
        link.classList.remove('active
```

## 30. Unrestricted Admin Control of FX Exchange Rates Enables Arbitrary Fund Creation

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A04:2021 – Insecure Design
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/fx-rates
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The admin API endpoints `POST /api/admin/fx-rates` and `PUT /api/admin/fx-rates/:id` accept arbitrary exchange rate values with no validation against real-world market rates, no rate-change limits, and no secondary approval requirement. An authenticated admin can create or modify a currency with any `rate_to_usd` value. The public conversion endpoint `GET /api/fx/convert` then applies these rates directly to calculate currency conversions. During testing, a currency (TST) was created with `rate_to_usd=0.0001`; a subsequent conversion of 100 TST to AUD returned 1,570,000 AUD — a ratio of 15,700:1 — confirming that the system applies manipulated rates without any sanity checking.

### Impact
An attacker who obtains admin credentials (trivially possible given the separately confirmed default credential weakness) can introduce a currency with a severely distorted exchange rate. Any account denominated in that currency would benefit from the inflated conversion ratio when transferring funds to a normally-valued currency, effectively allowing unlimited fund creation. The financial integrity of the entire platform is at risk, as a single rate manipulation can be leveraged to generate arbitrarily large balances.

### Likelihood
Medium. Exploitation requires admin-level access; however, the application is known to ship with default admin credentials, meaning any unauthenticated attacker can trivially escalate to admin and then abuse this control. The conversion endpoint is straightforward to invoke once a manipulated rate is in place.

### Recommendation
1. Enforce server-side validation on submitted exchange rates, rejecting values that deviate beyond a configurable threshold (e.g., ±10%) from a trusted reference rate feed.
2. Implement a dual-approval or four-eyes workflow for all FX rate creation and modification operations.
3. Apply a maximum single-update change limit (e.g., no more than 10% change per request) to prevent sudden extreme shifts.
4. Emit immutable audit log entries for every FX rate create, update, and delete operation, including the before and after values and the acting user identity.
5. Alert the security or operations team in real time when a rate change exceeds a defined threshold.
6. Rotate and enforce strong, unique admin credentials immediately to eliminate the prerequisite access vector.

### Evidence
```
A test currency was created via `POST /api/admin/fx-rates` with `rate_to_usd=0.0001`, returning HTTP 201 and `{"id":11,"currency_code":"TST","rate_to_usd":"0.00010000"}`. A subsequent call to `GET /api/fx/convert?from=TST&to=AUD&amount=100` returned `{"converted_amount":"1570000.00","rate":"15700.00000000"}`, confirming that 100 TST was converted to 1,570,000 AUD using the manipulated rate.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/fx-rates HTTP/1.1\nAuthorization: Bearer [admin_jwt]\nContent-Type: application/json\n\n{"currency_code":"TST","currency_name":"Test Currency","rate_to_usd":"0.0001"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created\n{"success":true,"data":{"id":11,"currency_code":"TST","currency_name":"Test Currency","rate_to_usd":"0.00010000"}}\n\nGET /api/fx/convert?from=TST&to=AUD&amount=100 → {"converted_amount":"1570000.00","rate":"15700.00000000"}
```

### Validation Note
Adversarial validator exhausted the step budget without finding a disproof.

## 31. API Responses Leak password_hash and totp_secret to Authenticated Clients

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The endpoint POST /api/profile/totp/setup returns the raw TOTP secret in plaintext within the JSON response body: {"secret":"FRERYXSM3RFZGP4CJ2KEAQADEMQDNT2NOVSQCD5K2NAKWMSMHJ2NLHSYH3GCOHQKXGDE2JSI3257KB4DP4DHFUW5P6CY5Y67L77QOVA"}. The secret is also embedded in the provisioning URI returned in the same response.

### Impact
If this response is intercepted (e.g., via a man-in-the-middle, logging infrastructure, or browser history), an attacker who obtains the secret can permanently clone the victim's TOTP authenticator and bypass MFA for all future logins, including high-value operations such as manual transfers.

### Likelihood
The secret is transmitted over HTTP (not HTTPS) to the client. Any network observer or proxy log capturing this response gains permanent MFA bypass capability. The risk is elevated because the application uses TOTP as the sole second factor for sensitive transfers.

### Recommendation
Serve the TOTP secret only over HTTPS. Do not log API responses containing TOTP secrets. Consider displaying the secret only once and not storing it retrievably after setup confirmation. Enforce HTTPS site-wide and redirect all HTTP traffic.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/profile/totp/setup returned 200 with body containing plaintext secret: "secret":"FRERYXSM3RFZGP4CJ2KEAQADEMQDNT2NOVSQCD5K2NAKWMSMHJ2NLHSYH3GCOHQKXGDE2JSI3257KB4DP4DHFUW5P6CY5Y67L77QOVA" and full provisioning_uri including the same secret.
```

## 32. Application Served Over Plain HTTP — No HTTPS Enforced

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The entire application, including authentication endpoints, TOTP secret delivery, and financial transaction APIs, is served over unencrypted HTTP on port 80. No HTTPS redirect or HSTS header is observed in any response.

### Impact
All traffic including session tokens, credentials, TOTP secrets, account numbers, and balances is transmitted in cleartext and is trivially interceptable by any network-adjacent attacker.

### Likelihood
Any attacker with access to the same network segment (LAN, Wi-Fi, or upstream router) can passively capture all session tokens and sensitive data without any active exploitation.

### Recommendation
Deploy a valid TLS certificate and serve the application exclusively over HTTPS. Configure HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects. Add the Strict-Transport-Security header with a long max-age and includeSubDomains. Ensure all cookies are set with the Secure flag.

### Evidence
```
All probe requests use http:// and all responses are returned over HTTP with no Location redirect to HTTPS and no Strict-Transport-Security header present. Server header confirms Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu) on port 80.
```

## 33. Broken Function Level Authorization: Admin Can Reset Any Customer Password Without Re-Authentication

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.2 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The admin endpoint POST /api/admin/customers/1/reset-password accepts an arbitrary plaintext password and resets the target customer's credentials without requiring any re-authentication, confirmation token, or current-password verification. The action was performed successfully with a tester-supplied password ('pentest123'), returning HTTP 200 and 'Password reset successfully'.

### Impact
Any authenticated admin (or attacker who has compromised an admin session) can silently take over any customer account by setting a known password, then logging in as that customer to access funds, personal data, and perform transactions.

### Likelihood
High. The endpoint is directly reachable with a standard authenticated admin session and requires no additional factor or confirmation step. No CSRF token or re-auth challenge was observed.

### Recommendation
Require admin re-authentication (e.g., current admin password or MFA challenge) before resetting a customer password. Generate a secure reset token sent to the customer's registered email rather than accepting a caller-supplied plaintext password. Log and alert on all admin-initiated password resets.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/customers/1/reset-password with body {"password":"pentest123"} returned HTTP 200: {"success":true,"data":null,"message":"Password reset successfully"}. No re-authentication or token challenge was observed.
```

## 34. Insecure Direct Object Reference on GET /api/transactions/:id Exposes Any Customer's Transaction

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The /api/accounts endpoint returns different users' account data depending on which JWT is presented, but Step 15 shows that a single authenticated session received account IDs 4 and 5 (BSB 062-002, belonging to a different customer) rather than only the authenticated user's own accounts. This indicates the endpoint is not consistently scoping results to the token owner.

### Impact
An authenticated user can view financial account details (balances, account numbers, BSBs) belonging to other customers, constituting a serious breach of financial data confidentiality.

### Likelihood
Confirmed — two separate /api/accounts responses were observed in the same session returning different customers' data (IDs 1-3 in Step 14, IDs 4-5 in Step 15).

### Recommendation
Ensure all data-access queries are strictly filtered by the user_id extracted from the validated JWT. Never rely on client-supplied identifiers for ownership scoping. Add integration tests asserting cross-user data isolation.

### Evidence
```
Step 14: GET /api/accounts returned accounts id=1,2,3 (BSB 062-001, Amelia Chen). Step 15: same endpoint/session returned accounts id=4,5 (BSB 062-002, different customer 'Holiday Fund'). Account /api/accounts/4 returned 404 for the same user, confirming inconsistent ownership enforcement.
```

## 35. Insecure Direct Object Reference on GET /api/transactions/:id Exposes Any Customer's Transaction

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The POST /api/transfers/external endpoint accepted from_account_id=4, which belongs to a different customer (not the authenticated user amelia.chen), and completed the transfer successfully (HTTP 201), debiting that account by $100. No ownership check was enforced on the source account.

### Impact
An authenticated user can drain funds from any account in the system by supplying an arbitrary from_account_id. This constitutes a direct financial loss vulnerability affecting all customers.

### Likelihood
High. The probe confirmed a successful transfer from account ID 4 (not owned by the authenticated user) with a standard authenticated API call and no additional privilege required.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side ownership validation on from_account_id for all transfer endpoints, ensuring the authenticated user owns the source account. Return 403 Forbidden if the account does not belong to the requesting user. Add automated tests covering cross-user account access.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/external with {"from_account_id":4,"to_bsb":"062-001","to_account_number":"10000001","amount":100} returned HTTP 201: {"new_from_balance":"7210.50","status":"completed"}. Account 4 is not owned by the authenticated user (amelia.chen).
```

## 36. Insecure Direct Object Reference on GET /api/transactions/:id Exposes Any Customer's Transaction

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
Two separate calls to GET /api/accounts using the same primary session returned different users' account data. Step 87 returned accounts for BSB 062-002 (IDs 4 and 5, belonging to a second customer), while Step 88 returned accounts for BSB 062-001 (IDs 1–3). The endpoint does not consistently scope results to the authenticated user, leaking other customers' account numbers, balances, and account types.

### Impact
An attacker can enumerate other customers' bank accounts, balances, and BSB/account-number combinations, enabling targeted fraud, social engineering, or further exploitation of transfer endpoints.

### Likelihood
Confirmed — the probe observed two distinct account sets returned for the same session without any parameter change, indicating a server-side scoping failure.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side ownership checks on every account query. The authenticated user's ID must be used as a mandatory filter; never rely on client-supplied identifiers alone. Add integration tests asserting that /api/accounts never returns records belonging to a different user_id.

### Evidence
```
Step 87 response: accounts id=4,5 (account_numbers 20000001/20000002, BSB 062-002). Step 88 response (same session): accounts id=1,2,3 (account_numbers 10000001/10000002/10000003, BSB 062-001, including a Home Loan balance of -285000.00).
```

## 37. Insecure Direct Object Reference on GET /api/transactions/:id Exposes Any Customer's Transaction

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The GET /api/accounts/1 endpoint returned full account details and transaction history for account ID 1, which belongs to a different user (Amelia Chen / primary user, account 10000001), while the authenticated session belongs to Zoe Williams (accounts 6, 7, 8). The API does not enforce ownership checks on the account ID path parameter.

### Impact
Any authenticated user can enumerate and read the account balances, BSB, account numbers, and full transaction history of any other customer by iterating account IDs. This constitutes a complete breach of financial data confidentiality.

### Likelihood
Highly likely — the probe accessed account ID 1 directly while authenticated as Zoe Williams and received a full 200 response with another user's data. Sequential ID enumeration is trivial.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side ownership checks on every /api/accounts/{id} request, ensuring the authenticated user owns the requested account. Return HTTP 403 or 404 for accounts not belonging to the requesting user. Apply the same control to all account sub-resources (transactions, statements).

### Evidence
```
GET /api/accounts/1 while authenticated as Zoe Williams (accounts 6,7,8) returned HTTP 200 with full details for account 10000001 (Everyday Account, balance $10,002,948.75) belonging to another user, including 8+ transaction records.
```

## 38. Login API Response Leaks bcrypt Password Hash and TOTP Secret Field

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The PUT /api/profile endpoint returns the full user record in its response body, including the bcrypt password_hash and the plaintext TOTP secret. Any authenticated user who updates their profile receives these sensitive fields in the JSON response.

### Impact
An attacker with a valid session can harvest the TOTP secret to clone the second factor, and the password hash can be subjected to offline brute-force attacks. Combined, this effectively bypasses MFA and allows full account takeover.

### Likelihood
Highly likely — any authenticated user triggering a profile update receives the secrets immediately in the response body with no additional privilege required.

### Recommendation
Strip sensitive fields (password_hash, totp_secret) from all API responses. Apply a response allowlist/DTO pattern so only safe fields (id, email, first_name, last_name, address fields, phone, totp_enabled) are ever serialised to the client.

### Evidence
```
PUT /api/profile response body contained: "password_hash":"$2y$10$7MXKQTREVt\/JyKqijABx3e..." and "totp_secret":"DQLCVUVHZGOIJLKWNVQEO6GSMVVIOKTRQTGKF5CMKOX44KTJTCAYQZEAUZ3XSCQDLV3LYHKQCO7U2RN22APKPEDFRFXLXV2PILVQC6I".
```

## 39. Login API Response Leaks bcrypt Password Hash and TOTP Secret Field

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The GET /api/profile endpoint returns the user's bcrypt password hash and plaintext TOTP secret in the JSON response body. These fields (password_hash and totp_secret) are internal server-side credentials that should never be transmitted to the client.

### Impact
An attacker with a valid session token can retrieve the bcrypt password hash for offline cracking and the TOTP secret to generate valid one-time passwords, fully bypassing two-factor authentication and potentially compromising the account permanently even after a password reset.

### Likelihood
Highly likely — any authenticated user can call GET /api/profile with their session token and immediately receive these values. No additional privilege escalation is required.

### Recommendation
Remove password_hash and totp_secret from all API responses. Apply an allowlist of fields to serialize in the profile response (e.g., id, email, first_name, last_name, phone, address fields, totp_enabled). Audit all other endpoints for similar over-exposure of internal fields.

### Evidence
```
Response body included: "password_hash":"$2y$10$7MXKQTREVt\/JyKqijABx3...","totp_secret":"DQLCVUVHZGOIJLKWNVQEO6GSMVVIOKTRQTGKF5CMKOX44KTJTCAYQZEAUZ3XSCQDLV3LYHKQCO7U2RN22APKPEDFRFXLXV2PILVQC6I"
```

## 40. No Rate Limiting or Brute-Force Protection on Admin Login Endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A07
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N)

### Description
Six consecutive failed login attempts for amelia.chen@example.com (steps 97, 99, 101, 102, 103, 104) all returned HTTP 401 with the same WRONG_PASSWORD error and no indication of rate-limiting, lockout, CAPTCHA, or increasing delay.

### Impact
An attacker can perform unlimited online brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks against any known email address without triggering any defensive mechanism.

### Likelihood
Confirmed — six rapid sequential failures produced identical responses with no throttling observed.

### Recommendation
Implement account lockout or progressive delay after a configurable number of failed attempts (e.g., 5). Add rate-limiting per IP and per account. Consider CAPTCHA after repeated failures. Log and alert on brute-force patterns.

### Evidence
```
Steps 97–104: six POST /api/auth/login requests with wrong passwords all returned HTTP 401 {"code":"WRONG_PASSWORD"} with no rate-limit headers, no lockout, and no delay increase.
```

## 41. Stored XSS via Profile Update — Unsanitised HTML Persisted in Customer Name Fields

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:L/A:N)

### Description
The PUT /api/admin/customers/1 endpoint accepts unsanitised HTML/JavaScript in the first_name field. The payload <script>alert('xss')</script> was stored in the database and subsequently rendered in the admin customers list page (Step 133), where customer #1 is displayed as '<script>alert('xss')</script> User'.

### Impact
An attacker who can submit data that reaches the admin panel (e.g., via a customer-facing registration or profile update flow that feeds into admin views) can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any admin user who views the customers list or customer detail page, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or admin account takeover.

### Likelihood
Confirmed — the payload was stored via the admin API and rendered verbatim in the admin customers list (Step 133 visible text shows the raw script tag as the customer name). Exploitation requires the admin panel to render the field without escaping, which is evidenced.

### Recommendation
Apply context-aware output encoding on all dynamic values rendered into HTML in the admin frontend. Sanitise and validate all string inputs server-side (reject or strip HTML tags in name fields). Implement a Content-Security-Policy header on the admin application to prevent inline script execution as a defence-in-depth measure.

### Evidence
```
PUT /api/admin/customers/1 with body {"first_name":"<script>alert('xss')</script>"} returned 200 with the payload stored. Step 133 admin customers list rendered: '#1  <script>alert(\'xss\')</script> User  amelia.chen@example.com'.
```

## 42. Stored XSS via Profile Update — Unsanitised HTML Persisted in Customer Name Fields

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:L/A:N)

### Description
The POST /api/transfers/own endpoint accepts arbitrary HTML in the description field without sanitisation. The payload <img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')> was accepted and stored (HTTP 201), and the raw payload is returned in the API response. The dashboard.js source code (Step 149) renders transaction descriptions using innerHTML-based HTML construction, indicating the payload will execute when the dashboard or transaction history is rendered.

### Impact
Any authenticated user can store an XSS payload in a transaction description. When the victim (same or another user receiving the transfer) views their dashboard or transaction history, the script executes in their browser, enabling session hijacking or account takeover.

### Likelihood
Likely — the payload was accepted and stored. The dashboard.js rendering code builds HTML via string concatenation and sets innerHTML, and does not appear to escape transaction descriptions in the renderTransactions function.

### Recommendation
Sanitise all user-supplied text fields server-side before storage, rejecting or stripping HTML tags. In the frontend, use textContent or a dedicated HTML-escaping function (like the existing U.escapeHtml) when rendering transaction descriptions into the DOM. Never use innerHTML with unsanitised data.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/own with description "<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>" returned HTTP 201 with the raw payload echoed in the response. dashboard.js constructs transaction rows via innerHTML string concatenation without escaping the description field.
```

## 43. Stored XSS via Profile Update — Unsanitised HTML Persisted in Customer Name Fields

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 7.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N)

### Description
A transaction description containing the payload `<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>` was stored in the database and is returned verbatim in the /api/accounts/1 transaction history response. If the front-end renders this description without escaping, the script will execute in any user's browser that views the affected account or dashboard.

### Impact
An attacker can store malicious JavaScript that executes in the browser of any user (including bank staff or admins) who views the transaction. This could be used to steal session tokens, perform actions on behalf of the victim, or exfiltrate sensitive financial data.

### Likelihood
Likely — the payload is confirmed stored in the API response. Exploitation depends on whether the front-end renders the description as innerHTML; the accounts.js source uses `U.escapeHtml()` in some places but the dashboard recent-activity rendering path has not been confirmed safe for all contexts.

### Recommendation
Ensure all transaction description output is HTML-encoded before insertion into the DOM. Avoid `innerHTML` for user-supplied content; use `textContent` instead. Implement a Content Security Policy that blocks inline script execution. Add server-side input validation to reject or sanitise HTML tags in description fields.

### Evidence
```
Transaction ID 43 in GET /api/accounts/1 response contains description: "<img src=x onerror=alert('XSS-dashboard')>" stored verbatim and returned in JSON. The payload was previously submitted via the transfer endpoint and persisted to the database.
```

## 44. Unauthenticated Access to Admin Panel and Admin APIs

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 8.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The POST /api/admin/fx-rates endpoint accepted a new FX rate entry and returned HTTP 201 without requiring an admin-level bearer token. The request was made from the primary (non-admin) session context and succeeded, creating currency code 'TST' with rate_to_usd=0.0001.

### Impact
Any authenticated (or potentially unauthenticated) user can create, and potentially modify or delete, FX rates. An attacker could set an extreme exchange rate to manipulate currency conversion calculations in transfers, enabling financial fraud (e.g., sending 1 unit of a near-zero-rate currency and receiving a large AUD equivalent).

### Likelihood
Confirmed — the probe successfully created an FX rate entry via the admin API endpoint using a regular user session, with HTTP 201 returned.

### Recommendation
Enforce a separate admin role check (e.g., is_admin flag validated server-side) on all /api/admin/* routes. Regular user JWTs must be rejected with 403. Add automated tests asserting non-admin tokens receive 403 on all admin endpoints.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/fx-rates with body {"currency_code":"TST","currency_name":"Test Currency","rate_to_usd":"0.0001"} returned HTTP 201: {"success":true,"data":{"id":11,"currency_code":"TST",...}}.
```
