## 45. API Responses Leak password_hash and totp_secret to Authenticated Clients

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/profile
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
Several API endpoints serialize and return sensitive security credential fields directly to the client in their JSON responses. Specifically, the bcrypt password hash (password_hash) and the raw TOTP seed (totp_secret) are included in responses from GET /api/profile, PUT /api/profile, and POST /api/auth/login. The raw TOTP secret is additionally returned by POST /api/profile/totp/setup. These fields have no legitimate use on the client side and must never leave the server.

### Impact
An attacker who obtains a valid session token — through theft, XSS, or any other means — can retrieve the victim's bcrypt password hash and submit it to offline cracking tools, recovering the plaintext password without any further interaction with the server. More critically, possession of the raw totp_secret allows the attacker to permanently clone the victim's TOTP authenticator: they can generate valid one-time codes at any future point in time, completely bypassing two-factor authentication on logins and sensitive operations even if the victim subsequently changes their password.

### Likelihood
Any authenticated user can trigger this exposure with a standard GET /api/profile request. No elevated privileges, special tooling, or race conditions are required. The fields are returned unconditionally in the normal application flow, making exploitation straightforward for any attacker who holds a valid bearer token.

### Recommendation
Strip password_hash and totp_secret from all serialised user objects before any API response is sent to the client. This is best enforced at the serialisation layer (e.g., via an allowlist of fields permitted in API output) rather than relying on per-endpoint exclusions. The TOTP setup endpoint (POST /api/profile/totp/setup) should return only the provisioning URI and QR code image data needed for initial authenticator enrollment; the raw secret must not be included in the response body after setup is complete. Conduct a full audit of all endpoints that return user objects to confirm no other sensitive fields are inadvertently exposed.

### Evidence
```
A GET /api/profile request with a valid bearer token returned a JSON body containing both password_hash ("$2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC/.og/at2.uheWG/igi") and totp_secret ("A2SZC3UPM6EUVFNFTZTKNP3EK5PYSXBZBY7SB5UBEPEJUPH5WZ6JC3RS6GYYXOZWYBEMZ46ECS5M6HLZCUEUSDVRGIFCZMIYWD4JVRQ"). The same fields were observed in PUT /api/profile and POST /api/auth/login responses. POST /api/profile/totp/setup additionally returned the raw secret alongside the provisioning URI.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /api/profile\nAuthorization: Bearer [token]
```

### Response Evidence
```
{"password_hash":"$2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC\/.og\/at2.uheWG\/igi","totp_secret":"A2SZC3UPM6EUVFNFTZTKNP3EK5PYSXBZBY7SB5UBEPEJUPH5WZ6JC3RS6GYYXOZWYBEMZ46ECS5M6HLZCUEUSDVRGIFCZMIYWD4JVRQ",...}
```

## 46. CORS Misconfiguration — Arbitrary Origin Reflected with Credentials Allowed

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/profile
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The API at http://192.168.3.101/api/profile (and consistently across all API endpoints) reflects the caller's Origin header verbatim in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header while simultaneously setting Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. When a request was sent with Origin: https://evil.example.com, the server responded with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, and returned the full authenticated profile payload — including the password_hash and totp_secret fields — in the response body.

### Impact
Because the server reflects arbitrary origins and permits credentialed cross-origin requests, any attacker-controlled website can issue authenticated API calls on behalf of a logged-in victim using the victim's existing session token or cookies, and read the full response in JavaScript. In this engagement the response body was confirmed to contain sensitive fields (password_hash, totp_secret). A victim who visits a malicious page while authenticated to the banking application could have their profile data, account balances, and transaction history silently exfiltrated. The severity is elevated above the default CORS baseline because browser-readable sensitive credential material (password hash, TOTP secret) was directly observed in the cross-origin response.

### Likelihood
A victim must be authenticated to the banking application and visit an attacker-controlled page in the same browser session. No additional user interaction beyond that single page visit is required, making the attack practical via phishing or malicious advertisement.

### Recommendation
1. Replace the dynamic origin reflection logic with an explicit server-side allowlist containing only the banking application's own trusted origin(s). Reject or omit the CORS headers entirely for any origin not on the allowlist.
2. Never combine a dynamically reflected or wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true — this combination is prohibited by the CORS specification for good reason.
3. If no cross-origin browser access is required, remove all CORS response headers from the API entirely.
4. Audit all API endpoints for the same pattern; evidence indicates the misconfiguration is present application-wide, not limited to /api/profile.
5. Ensure the API response never exposes raw password hashes or TOTP secrets to any client, regardless of CORS policy.

### Evidence
```
A GET request to /api/profile was issued with a spoofed Origin: https://evil.example.com header and a valid Bearer token. The server returned HTTP 200 with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, and the response body contained the authenticated user's profile including the fields password_hash and totp_secret. This behaviour was observed to be consistent across all API endpoints.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /api/profile HTTP/1.1\nOrigin: https://evil.example.com\nAuthorization: Bearer [token]
```

### Response Evidence
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com\nAccess-Control-Allow-Credentials: true\n\n{"password_hash":"$2y$10$...","totp_secret":null,...}
```

## 47. Insecure Direct Object Reference on GET /api/transactions/:id Exposes Any Customer's Transaction

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/transactions/1
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The GET /api/transactions/:id endpoint accepts a numeric transaction ID in the URL path but performs no server-side check to confirm that the requested transaction belongs to the authenticated user. Any valid session token is sufficient to retrieve the full details of any transaction in the system by iterating the integer ID. The response includes the source account identifier, destination BSB and account number, transfer amount, free-text description, and TOTP verification status.

### Impact
Any authenticated customer can enumerate all transaction IDs and read the complete financial activity of every other customer in the system. This constitutes full cross-customer exposure of sensitive payment data, including counterparty account details and transaction descriptions, without requiring any elevated privilege.

### Likelihood
High. Exploitation requires only a valid user account and the ability to increment an integer in a URL. No brute-force resistance, rate limiting, or ownership validation was observed on the endpoint. Two distinct test accounts were each able to retrieve transactions belonging to the other with a single unauthenticated-equivalent request.

### Recommendation
Implement a server-side ownership check on every request to GET /api/transactions/:id. Before returning a record, verify that the transaction's from_account_id is associated with an account owned by the identity in the authenticated JWT (sub claim). If the transaction does not belong to the requesting user, return HTTP 403 or HTTP 404 — do not return the record. This check must be enforced in the application layer and cannot rely on the client supplying a correct identifier.

### Evidence
```
Two test accounts were used to confirm bidirectional cross-customer access. User 2 (Wei Zhang, sub:2) requested transaction ID 1, which belongs to User 1 (Amelia Chen, from_account_id:1), and received HTTP 200 with full details: amount $500.00, description 'Monthly savings', destination BSB 062-001, account 10000002. User 1 (Amelia Chen, sub:1) requested transaction ID 10, which belongs to a third account (from_account_id:9), and received HTTP 200 with amount $400.00, description 'Mum allowance', destination BSB 063-100, account 44556677. In both cases the server returned complete transaction records for transactions the requesting user did not own.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /api/transactions/1 HTTP/1.1\nAuthorization: Bearer [user2_token]\n\nGET /api/transactions/10 HTTP/1.1\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_token]
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200: {"id":1,"from_account_id":1,"to_bsb":"062-001","to_account_number":"10000002","to_account_id":2,"amount":"500.00","description":"Monthly savings",...}\nHTTP 200: {"id":10,"from_account_id":9,"to_bsb":"063-100","to_account_number":"44556677","amount":"400.00","description":"Mum allowance",...}
```

## 48. Login API Response Leaks bcrypt Password Hash and TOTP Secret Field

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/login
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The POST /api/auth/login endpoint returns the authenticated user's internal database fields — including `password_hash` (a bcrypt hash) and `totp_secret` — directly in the JSON response body. These fields serve no legitimate client-side purpose and should never leave the server. The issue was confirmed across three separate accounts: amelia.chen@example.com, wei.zhang@example.com, and noah.campbell@example.com, indicating this is a systemic serialization problem rather than an isolated case.

### Impact
An attacker who obtains a login response — through network interception, XSS, a compromised client device, or any other means — receives the target user's bcrypt password hash. This hash can be subjected to offline dictionary or brute-force attacks without any further interaction with the server, bypassing account lockout and rate-limiting controls entirely. If `totp_secret` were populated (it is currently null for observed accounts), an attacker could derive the same time-based one-time passwords as the legitimate user, fully cloning the MFA device and negating the second authentication factor.

### Likelihood
High. Every successful login unconditionally returns the hash. Any session token theft, man-in-the-middle position, or client-side script injection that can read the response body immediately yields the hash. No elevated privilege or special condition is required beyond completing a normal login.

### Recommendation
Implement a dedicated API response DTO (Data Transfer Object) or serialization allowlist for the user object returned to clients. The response should include only fields required by the UI: id, email, first_name, last_name, phone, and totp_enabled. Fields such as `password_hash`, `totp_secret`, and any other internal or credential-bearing columns must be explicitly excluded at the serialization layer — not filtered conditionally — so that future schema additions do not inadvertently leak new sensitive fields. Audit all other authenticated endpoints that return user objects to confirm the same fix is applied consistently.

### Evidence
```
A POST to /api/auth/login with valid credentials for amelia.chen@example.com returned HTTP 200 with the following JSON body, which includes the bcrypt password hash and a totp_secret key: {"success":true,"data":{"user":{"id":1,"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","first_name":"Amelia","last_name":"Chen",...,"password_hash":"$2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC/.og/at2.uheWG/igi","totp_secret":null},"token":"eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..."}}. The same behaviour was confirmed for wei.zhang@example.com and noah.campbell@example.com.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/auth/login HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","password":"password"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
{"success":true,"data":{"user":{"id":1,"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","first_name":"Amelia","last_name":"Chen",...,"password_hash":"$2y$10$92IXUNpkjO0rOQ5byMi.Ye4oKoEa3Ro9llC\/.og\/at2.uheWG\/igi","totp_secret":null},"token":"eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..."}}
```

## 49. No Rate Limiting or Brute-Force Protection on Admin Login Endpoint

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/auth/login
- CVSS: 7.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The admin login endpoint at `/api/admin/auth/login` does not enforce any rate limiting, account lockout, or brute-force protection. Six consecutive authentication attempts using incorrect passwords (`wrongpass1` through `wrongpass6`) were submitted without any throttling, delay, or lockout being triggered. Each request returned an identical HTTP 401 response with no `Retry-After`, `X-RateLimit-*`, or similar protective headers present.

### Impact
An unauthenticated attacker can submit an unlimited number of login attempts against the admin endpoint without restriction. This makes the endpoint fully susceptible to automated brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks. If the admin account uses a weak or previously breached password, an attacker could gain full administrative access to the application.

### Likelihood
High. The endpoint is unauthenticated and publicly reachable, requires no prior knowledge beyond the URL, and imposes no observable constraint on request volume. Automated tooling can trivially enumerate passwords at high speed.

### Recommendation
Implement the following controls on the admin login endpoint: (1) rate limiting, such as a maximum of 5 failed attempts per source IP per 15-minute window, returning HTTP 429 with a `Retry-After` header; (2) progressive delays after repeated failures; (3) temporary account lockout after a configurable threshold of consecutive failures; (4) CAPTCHA or a similar challenge mechanism to deter automated submissions; and (5) centralised logging and alerting on anomalous authentication failure patterns to support detection and incident response.

### Evidence
```
Six consecutive POST requests to `/api/admin/auth/login` using passwords `wrongpass1` through `wrongpass6` each returned HTTP 401 with an identical response body and consistent response times. No `Retry-After`, `X-RateLimit-*`, lockout message, CAPTCHA challenge, or progressive delay was observed across any of the six attempts.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/admin/auth/login x6 with wrong passwords — no lockout or throttling observed
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 401 {"success":false,"error":{"code":"INVALID_CREDENTIALS","message":"Invalid username or password."}} — identical response for all 6 attempts, no rate-limit headers
```

## 50. Registration Endpoint Hashes Passwords with Unsalted MD5, Hash Exposed in API Response

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/register
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N)

### Description
The user registration endpoint at `POST /api/auth/register` stores newly created passwords using unsalted MD5 rather than a modern password-hashing algorithm. Existing seeded users have their passwords protected with bcrypt (`$2y$10$...`), indicating the registration code path diverges from the rest of the application. Compounding the issue, the computed hash is returned in plaintext within the HTTP 201 registration response body and is also present in subsequent profile API responses (e.g., `GET /api/profile`), making it trivially accessible to any client that registers or retrieves a user profile.

### Impact
An attacker who obtains the `password_hash` field — which is actively disclosed in API responses — can immediately reverse common passwords using publicly available MD5 rainbow tables or online lookup services, or crack arbitrary passwords in seconds using GPU-based tools. Because MD5 is unsalted, identical passwords produce identical hashes, enabling bulk cracking across all affected accounts simultaneously. This effectively eliminates password confidentiality for every user registered through the standard sign-up flow.

### Likelihood
High. The MD5 hash is returned directly in the registration and profile API responses without requiring any additional exploitation step. No authentication, elevated privilege, or special access is needed to obtain the hash for a newly registered account. Reversing MD5 hashes for common passwords requires no specialist capability.

### Recommendation
1. Replace MD5 with a purpose-built password hashing algorithm — bcrypt (cost factor ≥ 12), scrypt, or Argon2id — in the registration code path, consistent with the algorithm already used for seeded users.
2. Audit all code paths that handle password storage to ensure no other endpoints use MD5 or SHA-1.
3. Remove `password_hash` (and any other credential-derived fields) from all API responses, including registration, login, and profile endpoints — hashed passwords should never be returned to clients.
4. Identify all accounts created via the vulnerable registration path and force a password reset, as their stored hashes must be considered compromised.
5. After remediation, re-hash credentials at next login for any accounts that could not be reached by a forced reset.

### Evidence
```
Registering a new account via `POST /api/auth/register` with the password `TestPass123!` returned the HTTP 201 response body containing `"password_hash": "480341a0aa25a0d5697a606d49517dfd"` — a 32-character lowercase hex string, which is the MD5 digest of the submitted password rather than a bcrypt hash. The same hash was confirmed present in the `GET /api/profile` response for the newly created user (ID 16). By contrast, pre-existing seeded users carry bcrypt hashes in the `$2y$10$...` format, confirming the inconsistency is isolated to the registration code path.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/auth/register\n\n{"email":"test.user.final@test.com","password":"TestPass123!","first_name":"Test","last_name":"User"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 201: {"password_hash":"480341a0aa25a0d5697a606d49517dfd",...} — MD5 hash, not bcrypt
```

## 51. SQL Injection in Admin Customer Search Parameter

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A03:2021 – Injection
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/admin/customers
- CVSS: 8.8 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The `search` query parameter accepted by `GET /api/admin/customers` is concatenated directly into a MySQL query without parameterization or escaping. Submitting a single quote (`'`) as part of the search value breaks the query syntax, triggering a MySQL error that is returned verbatim in the HTTP 500 response. The error message exposes the raw SQL fragment, the server-side file path (`/var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php`), the line number, and a partial stack trace, confirming classic error-based SQL injection.

### Impact
An attacker who holds a valid admin session token can exploit this injection point to read arbitrary data from the database, including customer PII, account balances, transaction history, and password hashes. The verbose error response additionally discloses internal file paths and application structure, reducing the effort required to develop further exploits.

### Likelihood
Exploitation requires a valid admin-level Bearer token. An attacker who has obtained or compromised admin credentials can immediately leverage this vulnerability to extract the full database contents. No additional preconditions or complex techniques are needed beyond issuing a crafted GET request.

### Recommendation
1. Replace all dynamic SQL string concatenation with parameterized queries (PDO prepared statements with bound parameters) throughout the application, starting with `AdminUserController.php` line 28. 2. Audit all other controller endpoints for the same pattern. 3. Suppress detailed error messages, stack traces, and file paths from API responses in production environments — capture this information in server-side logs only and return a generic error message to the client.

### Evidence
```
Sending `GET /api/admin/customers?search=admin'` with a valid admin Bearer token returned HTTP 500 with a MySQL syntax error confirming unsanitized input injection: `SQLSTATE[42000]: Syntax error or access violation: 1064 You have an error in your SQL syntax ... near '%' OR last_name LIKE '%admin'%' OR email LIKE '%admin'%''`. The response also disclosed the source file path `/var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php` at line 28 and a PDO stack trace.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /api/admin/customers?search=admin' HTTP/1.1\nAuthorization: Bearer [admin_token]
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 500\n{"success":false,"error":{"code":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"SQLSTATE[42000]: Syntax error or access violation: 1064 You have an error in your SQL syntax; check the manual that corresponds to your MySQL server version for the right syntax to use near '%' OR last_name LIKE '%admin'%' OR email LIKE '%admin'%'' at line 1","details":{"file":"/var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php","line":28,"trace":"#0 /var/www/bankofed/src/Controllers/AdminUserController.php(28): PDO->query()..."}}}
```

## 52. Unrestricted Loan Account Drawdown via Transfer Endpoints

- Severity: high
- OWASP: A04:2021 – Insecure Design
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/transfers/own
- CVSS: 8.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H)

### Description
The transfer endpoints at /api/transfers/own and /api/transfers/external perform no server-side validation of the source account type. A customer can specify a loan account (e.g., account_id 3, a Home Loan) as the from_account in a transfer request, causing the loan balance to be debited — effectively drawing down on the loan — without any redraw eligibility check, approved redraw limit, credit assessment, or bank authorisation. This behaviour is present for both internal (own-account) and external transfers, meaning loan funds can be directed to third-party accounts with no controls applied.

### Impact
An authenticated customer can unilaterally increase their loan liability to an arbitrary amount by repeatedly initiating transfers from a loan account. This bypasses all redraw controls, credit limits, and approval workflows that would ordinarily govern loan drawdowns. The bank bears the resulting financial exposure, as funds are disbursed without any corresponding approval or eligibility verification. External transfers extend this risk to third-party beneficiaries, making recovery more difficult.

### Likelihood
High. The attack requires only a valid customer session and knowledge of the loan account ID, both of which are available to any legitimate account holder. No elevated privileges, special tooling, or complex exploitation chain is required — a standard authenticated API call is sufficient.

### Recommendation
Implement server-side account-type validation within both transfer handlers. Loan accounts must not be accepted as source accounts for customer-initiated transfers unless a separate, explicitly approved redraw facility is in place. Specifically: (1) reject any transfer request where the source account type is 'loan' by default; (2) introduce a redraw eligibility check that verifies an approved redraw limit exists before permitting drawdown; (3) enforce server-side redraw limit caps so that cumulative drawdowns cannot exceed the approved facility; (4) route any redraw request through an appropriate authorisation workflow rather than processing it inline with standard transfers. These controls must be enforced on the server — client-side restrictions alone are insufficient.

### Evidence
```
Using an authenticated session (user1_token), a POST request to /api/transfers/own specified loan account_id 3 (Home Loan, balance –$285,000) as the source. The server returned HTTP 201 and recorded a new loan balance of –$285,100, confirming $100 was drawn from the loan and credited to savings account_id 1 with no approval or eligibility check. A subsequent POST to /api/transfers/external using the same loan account as source returned HTTP 201 with a new balance of –$285,600, demonstrating that $500 was transferred externally from the loan account. In both cases the response status was 'completed' and no redraw validation was performed.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/transfers/own\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_token]\n\n{"amount":100,"from_account_id":3,"to_account_id":1,"transfer_type":"own","description":"loan account source test"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 201: {"transaction_id":37,"from_account_id":3,"to_account_id":1,"amount":"100","new_from_balance":"-285100.00","new_to_balance":"3549.75","status":"completed"}
```

## 53. Sensitive data exposed in API response

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A02
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 6.5

### Description
The response contains field names commonly associated with secrets, hashes, tokens, debug state, or privileged metadata.

### Impact
Attackers can use leaked secrets or implementation details to compromise accounts or chain further attacks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic response analysis.

### Recommendation
Remove sensitive fields from client-facing responses and enforce response DTO allow-lists.

### Evidence
```

x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
access-control-allow-origin: *
access-control-allow-credentials: true
access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD
access-control-allow-headers: *
access-control-max-age: 86400
content-length: 280
keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8

{"success":true,"data":{"status":"ok","php_version":"8.3.31","server":"Apache\/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)","db_host":"127.0.0.1","db_name":"bankofed","db_user":"bankofed_app","jwt_secret":"u8CX2709PakoFlIGDxrtHK3CAiuMURdVfp6aPVsiecFxPfyLtU9ku7eOw","environment":"production"},"message":"OK"}
```

The response contains field names commonly associated with secrets, hashes, tokens, debug state, or privileged metadata.

RESPONSE:
Status: 200

## 57. Sensitive data exposed in API response

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A02
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/auth/register
- CVSS: 6.5

### Description
The response contains field names commonly associated with secrets, hashes, tokens, debug state, or privileged metadata.

### Impact
Attackers can use leaked secrets or implementation details to compromise accounts or chain further attacks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic response analysis.

### Recommendation
Remove sensitive fields from client-facing responses and enforce response DTO allow-lists.

window.BankOfEd = window.BankOfEd || {};

BankOfEd.App = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var U = BankOfEd.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEd.Api;
  var Router = BankOfEd.Router;

  function init() {
    // Register routes
    Router.addRoute('/login', function () {
      BankOfEd.AuthPage.show();
    }, { auth: false });

    Router.addRoute('/register', function () {
      BankOfEd.AuthPage.show();
      BankOfEd.AuthPage.toggleTab('register');
    }, { auth: false });

    Router.addRoute('/dashboard', function () {
      BankOfEd.DashboardPage.show();
    });

## 59. Unauthenticated access to protected endpoint

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A01
- Source: Deterministic
- Validation: confirmed
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/app.js
- CVSS: 6.5

### Description
The deterministic auth matrix requested a protected or sensitive-looking endpoint without cookies or Authorization and received a successful response.

### Impact
Attackers may be able to access protected application functionality or sensitive operational data without the intended authentication or role checks.

### Likelihood
Confirmed by deterministic auth matrix request.

### Recommendation
Enforce server-side authentication and authorization on the endpoint. Do not rely on client-side route hiding or UI controls.

### Evidence
```

REQUEST:
GET http://192.168.3.101/admin/js/app.js HTTP/1.1
Actor: anonymous
Authorization: none

RESPONSE:
HTTP/1.1 200
date: Sun, 31 May 2026 01:36:13 GMT
server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu)
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-frame-options: DENY
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
last-modified: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 03:13:53 GMT
etag: "7cd-64afc760c5e40-gzip"

vary: Accept-Encoding

keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: text/javascript

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - App Bootstrap
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.App = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;
  var Router = BankOfEdAdmin.Router;

  function init() {
    Router.addRoute('/login', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.AuthPage.show(); }, { auth: false });
    Router.addRoute('/customers', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.CustomersPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/customers/:id', function (params) { BankOfEdAdmin.CustomersPage.showDetail(params); });
    Router.addRoute('/accounts', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.AccountsPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/fx-rates', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.FxRatesPage.show(); });
    Router.addRoute('/system', function () { BankOfEdAdmin.SystemPage.show(); });

    if (Api.isLoggedIn()) updateSidebar();
    Router.start();
  }

  function updateSidebar() {
    var admin = Api.getAdmin();
    if (!admin) return;
    var avatar = U.$('sidebar-avatar');
    var name = U.$('sidebar-admin-name');
    if (avatar) avatar.textContent = (admin.username || 'A')[0].toUpperCase();
    if (name) name.textContent = admin.username || 'Admin';
  }

  function toggleSidebar() {
    var sidebar = U.$('sidebar');
    var overlay = U.$('sidebar-overlay');
    if (sidebar.classList.contains('-translate-x-full')) {
      sidebar.classList.remove('-translate-x-full');
      overlay.classList.remove('hidden');
    } else {
      sidebar.classList.add('-translate-x-full');
      overlay.classList.add('hidden');
    }
  }

  function logout() {
    Api.logout().catch(function () {}).finally(function () {
      Api.clearToken(); Api.clearAdmin();
      U.toast('Signed out.', 'info');
      window.location.hash = '#/login';
    });
  }

  if (document.readyState === 'loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
  else init();

  return { init: init, updateSidebar: updateSidebar, toggleSidebar: toggleSidebar, logout: logout };
})();
```

/**

 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.AccountsPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;

  function handleLogin(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var form = e.target;
    var btn = form.querySelector('button[type="submit"]');
    var data = U.getFormData(form);
    U.showErrors('login-errors', null);
    U.setButtonLoading(btn, true);

    Api.login(data).then(function (res) {
      Api.setToken(res.data.token);
      Api.setAdmin(res.data.admin);
      U.toast('Welcome, ' + res.data.admin.username + '!', 'success');
      window.location.hash = '#/customers';
    }).catch(function (err) {
      U.showErrors('login-errors', err);
    }).finally(function () {
      U.setButtonLoading(btn, false);
    });
  }

  return { show: show, handleLogin: handleLogin };
})();
```

          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-dark-600">' + U.escapeHtml(rate.currency_name) + '</td>' +
          '<td class="px-6 py-3.5 text-right font-mono text-dark-900">' + Number(rate.rate_to_usd).toFixed(8) + '</td>' +

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - System Page
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.SystemPage = (function () {
  'use strict';
  var U = BankOfEdAdmin.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEdAdmin.Api;

  function show() {
    BankOfEdAdmin.Router.showPage('system', 'system');
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (input) input.value = '';
  }

  function handleReset() {
    var input = U.$('reset-confirm-input');
    if (!input || input.value !== 'RESET') {
      U.toast('Please type RESET to confirm.', 'warning');
      return;
    }

    var btn = U.$('reset-btn');
    U.setButtonLoading(btn, true);

    Api.resetDatabase().then(function () {
      input.value = '';
      U.toast('Database reset successfully!', 'success');
    }).catch(function (err) {
      U.toast(err.message || 'Reset failed.', 'error');
    }).finally(function () {
      U.setButtonLoading(btn, false);
    });
  }

  return { show: show, handleReset: handleReset };
})();

/**
 * BankOfEd Admin - Router
 */
window.BankOfEdAdmin = window.BankOfEdAdmin || {};

BankOfEdAdmin.Router = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var routes = [];

  function addRoute(pattern, handler, options) {
    options = options || {};
    var keys = [];
    var re = pattern.replace(/:([^/]+)/g, function (_, key) { keys.push(key); return '([^/]+)'; });
    routes.push({ regex: new RegExp('^' + re + '$'), keys: keys, handler: handler, auth: options.auth !== false });
  }

  function navigate(hash) { window.location.hash = hash; }

  function resolve() {
    var hash = window.location.hash.slice(1) || '/login';
    for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
      var route = routes[i];
      var match = hash.match(route.regex);
      if (match) {
        if (route.auth && !BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn()) { navigate('#/login'); return; }
        if (!route.auth && BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() && hash === '/login') { navigate('#/customers'); return; }
        var params = {};
        route.keys.forEach(function (key, idx) { params[key] = match[idx + 1]; });
        route.handler(params);
        return;
      }
    }
    navigate(BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() ? '#/customers' : '#/login');
  }

  function showPage(pageId, navKey) {
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="page-"]').forEach(function (p) { p.classList.add('hidden'); });
    var page = document.getElementById('page-' + pageId);
    if (page) { page.classList.remove('hidden'); page.classList.add('page-fade-in'); setTimeout(function () { page.classList.remove('page-fade-in'); }, 250); }

    var viewAuth = document.getElementById('view-auth');
    var appShell = document.getElementById('app-shell');
    if (pageId === 'auth') { viewAuth.classList.remove('hidden'); appShell.classList.add('hidden'); }
    else { viewAuth.classList.add('hidden'); appShell.classList.remove('hidden'); }

    if (navKey) {
      document.querySelectorAll('.nav-link').forEach(function (link) {
        link.classList.remove('active
```

    routes.push({ regex: new RegExp('^' + re + '$'), keys: keys, handler: handler, auth: options.auth !== false });
  }

  function navigate(hash) { window.location.hash = hash; }

  function resolve() {
    var hash = window.location.hash.slice(1) || '/login';
    for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
      var route = routes[i];
      var match = hash.match(route.regex);
      if (match) {
        if (route.auth && !BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn()) { navigate('#/login'); return; }
        if (!route.auth && BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() && hash === '/login') { navigate('#/customers'); return; }
        var params = {};
        route.keys.forEach(function (key, idx) { params[key] = match[idx + 1]; });
        route.handler(params);
        return;
      }
    }
    navigate(BankOfEdAdmin.Api.isLoggedIn() ? '#/customers' : '#/login');
  }

  function showPage(pageId, navKey) {
    document.querySelectorAll('[id^="page-"]').forEach(function (p) { p.classList.add('hidden'); });
    var page = document.getElementById('page-' + pageId);
    if (page) { page.classList.remove('hidden'); page.classList.add('page-fade-in'); setTimeout(function () { page.classList.remove('page-fade-in'); }, 250); }

    var viewAuth = document.getElementById('view-auth');
    var appShell = document.getElementById('app-shell');
    if (pageId === 'auth') { viewAuth.classList.remove('hidden'); appShell.classList.add('hidden'); }
    else { viewAuth.classList.add('hidden'); appShell.classList.remove('hidden'); }

  function formatDate(iso) {
    if (!iso) return '—';
    return new Date(iso).toLocaleDateString('en-AU', { day: 'numeric', month: 'short', year: 'numeric' });
  }

  }

  function toast(message, type) {
    type = type || 'info';
    var container = document.getElementById('toast-container');
    var colors = { success: 'bg-green-600', error: 'bg-red-600', info: 'bg-dark-700', warning: 'bg-amber-500' };
    var el = document.createElement('div');
    el.className = 'toast-enter flex items-center gap-3 px-5 py-3.5 rounded-xl text-white shadow-lg text-sm max-w-sm ' + (colors[type] || colors.info);
    el.innerHTML = '<span>' + escapeHtml(message) + '</span>';
    container.appendChild(el);
    setTimeout(function () {
      el.classList.remove('toast-enter');
      el.classList.add('toast-exit');
      el.addEventListener('animationend', function () { el.remove(); });
    }, 3500);
  }

  function openModal(html) {
    var overlay = document.getElementById('modal-overlay');
    document.getElementById('modal-content').innerHTML = html;
    overlay.classList.remove('hidden');
    requestAnimationFrame(function () { overlay.classList.add('show'); });
    overlay.onclick = function (e) { if (e.target === overlay) closeModal(); };
    document._modalEsc = function (e) { if (e.key === 'Escape') closeModal(); };
    document.addEventListener('keydown', document._modalEsc);
  }

    var overlay = document.getElementById('modal-ov

/**
 * BankOfEd - Accounts Page
 */
window.BankOfEd = window.BankOfEd || {};

BankOfEd.AccountsPage = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var U = BankOfEd.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEd.Api;

  function show() {
    BankOfEd.Router.showPage('accounts', 'accounts');
    loadAccounts();
  }

  function loadAccounts() {
    Api.getAccounts().then(function (res) {
      var accounts = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : (res.data.accounts || []);
      renderList(accounts);
    }).catch(function () {
      U.toast('Failed to load accounts.', 'error');
    });
  }

  function renderList(accounts) {
    var list = U.$('accounts-list');
    var empty = U.$('accounts-empty');

    if (!accounts.length) {
      list.innerHTML = '';
      empty.classList.remove('hidden');
      return;
    }

    empty.classList.add('hidden');
    var html = '';
    accounts.forEach(function (acc) {
      var balanceColor = parseFloat(acc.balance) >= 0 ? 'text-slate-900' : 'text-red-600';
      var currency = acc.currency || 'AUD';
      html +=
        '<a href="#/accounts/' + acc.id + '" class="account-card bg-white rounded-2xl shadow-sm border border-slate-100 p-5 flex items-center justify-between gap-4 block">' +
          '<div class="flex-1 min-w-0">' +
            '<div class="flex items-center gap-3 mb-1">' +
              '<h3 class="font-semibold text-slate-900 truncate">' + U.escapeHtml(acc.account_name) + '</h3>' +
              U.accountTypeBadge(acc.account_type, currency) +
            '</div>' +
            '<p class="text-sm text-slate-400 font-mono">BSB: ' + U.escapeHtml(acc.bsb) + ' &nbsp; Acc: ' + U.escapeHtml(acc.account_number) + '</p>' +
          '</div>' +
          '<div class="text-right flex-shrink-0">' +
            '<p class="text-xl font-bold ' + balanceColor + '">' + U.formatCurrency(acc.balance, currency) + '</p>' +
            '<p class="text-xs text-slate-400 mt-1">Available</p>' +
          '</div>' +
          '<svg class="w-5 h-5 text-slate-300 flex-shrink-0"
```

    U.$('profile-phone').value = user.phone || '';

    U.$('profile-suburb').value = user.suburb || '';
    U.$('profile-state').value = user.state || '';
    U.$('profile-postcode').value = user.postcode || '';
  }

  function toggleEdit() {
    if (isEditing) {
      cancelEdit();
    } else {
      isEditing = true;
      setFieldsEditable(true);
      U.$('profile-actions').classList.remove('hidden');
      U.$('profile-edit-btn').textContent = 'Cancel';
    }
  }

  function cancelEdit() {
    isEditing = false;
    setFieldsEditable(false);
    U.$('profile-actions').classList.add('hidden');
    U.$('profile-edit-btn').textContent = 'Edit';
    U.showErrors('profile-errors', null);
    if (currentProfile) populateProfile(currentProfile);

/**
 * BankOfEd - Transfers Page
 */
window.BankOfEd = window.BankOfEd || {};

BankOfEd.TransfersPage = (function () {
  'use strict';

  var U = BankOfEd.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEd.Api;

  var accounts = [];
  var addressBook = [];
  var currentTab = 'own';
  var payeeMethod = 'addressbook';
  var totpCheckResult = null;
  var convertTimer = null;

  function show() {
    BankOfEd.Router.showPage('transfers', 'transfers');
    currentTab = 'own';
    payeeMethod = 'addressbook';
    totpCheckResult = null;

    // Reset UI
    switchTab('own');
    setPayeeMethod('addressbook');
    hideTotpSections();

    // Reset forms
    var ownForm = U.$('transfer-own-form');
    var extForm = U.$('transfer-ext-form');
    if (ownForm) ownForm.reset();
    if (extForm) extForm.reset();
    U.showErrors('transfer-own-errors', null);
    U.showErrors('transfer-ext-errors', null);

    // Load data
    Api.getAccounts().then(function (res) {
      accounts = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : (res.data.accounts || []);
      populateOwnDropdowns();
      populateExtFromDropdown();
    });

    Api.getAddressBook().then(function (res) {
      addressBook = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : (res.data.entries || []);
      populatePayeeDropdown();
    });

    // BSB formatting
    var bsbInput = U.$('transfer-ext-bsb');
    if (bsbInput && !bsbInput._bsbFormatted) {
      U.formatBsbInput(bsbInput);
      bsbInput._bsbFormatted = true;
    }
  }

  function switchTab(tab) {
    currentTab = tab;
    var ownTab = U.$('transfer-tab-own');
    var extTab = U.$('transfer-tab-external');
    var ownPanel = U.$('transfer-own-panel');
    var extPanel = U.$('transfer-external-panel');

    if (tab === 'external') {
      ownTab.className = 'flex-1 py-4 text-center font-semibold text-slate-400 border-b-2 border-transparent hover:text-slate-600 transition-colors';
      extTab.className = 'flex-1 py-4 text-center font-semibold text-blue-600 border-b-2 border-blue-600 transition-color

  'use strict';

  var U = BankOfEd.Utils;
  var Api = BankOfEd.Api;

  var accounts = [];
  var addressBook = [];
  var currentTab = 'own';
  var payeeMethod = 'addressbook';
  var totpCheckResult = null;
  var convertTimer = null;

    currentTab = 'own';
    payeeMethod = 'addressbook';
    totpCheckResult = null;

    // Reset UI
    switchTab('own');
    setPayeeMethod('addressbook');
    hideTotpSections();

    var extForm = U.$('transfer-ext-form');

    U.showErrors('transfer-ext-errors', null);

    // Load data
    Api.getAccounts().then(function (res) {
      accounts = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : (res.data.accounts || []);
      populateOwnDropdowns();
      populateExtFromDropdown();
    });

    Api.getAddressBook().then(function (res) {
      addressBook = Array.isArray(res.data) ? res.data : (res.data.entries || []);
      populatePayeeDropdown();
    });

    // BSB formatting
    var bsbInput = U.$('transfer-ext-bsb');
    if (bsbInput && !bsbInput._bsbFormatted) {
      U.formatBsbInput(bsbInput);
      bsbInput._bsbFormatted = true;
    }
  }

  function switchTab(tab) {
    currentTab = tab;
    var ownTab = U.$('transfer-tab-own');
    var extTab = U.$('transfer-tab-external');
    var ownPanel = U.$('transfer-own-panel');
    var extPanel = U.$('transfer-external-panel');

    if (tab === 'external') {
      ownTab.className = 'flex-1 py-4 text-center font-semibold text-slate-400 border-b-2 border-transparent hover:text-slate-600 transition-colors';
      extTab.className = 'flex-1 py-4 text-center font-semibold text-blue-600 border-b-2 border-blue-600 transition-color
```

## 70. No Password Complexity Enforcement at Registration

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A04
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 5.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N)

### Description
The /api/auth/register endpoint accepts and successfully creates accounts with trivially weak passwords such as 'a' (single character) and '123' (three characters), returning HTTP 201 in both cases. No minimum length or complexity policy is enforced.

### Impact
Users can set passwords that are trivially guessable or brute-forceable. Combined with the MD5 hashing issue, even a hashed weak password is cracked instantly. This significantly lowers the bar for account takeover.

### Likelihood
Certain — both single-character and three-character passwords were accepted without error in Steps 118 and 119.

### Recommendation
Enforce a minimum password length of at least 12 characters and reject passwords found in common breach lists (e.g., using the HIBP API or a local blocklist). Return a clear validation error when the policy is not met.

### Evidence
```
Step 118: password 'a' accepted, HTTP 201 returned. Step 119: password '123' accepted, HTTP 201 returned. No validation error was produced in either case.

## 71. Verbose Internal Error Disclosure in Admin API Responses

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 5.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
When requests are made to authenticated endpoints without a valid token, the API returns full PHP stack traces including absolute file paths (/var/www/bankofed/src/...), class names, method signatures, and line numbers in the JSON error response.

### Impact
An attacker gains detailed knowledge of the server's directory structure, framework internals, and code flow, which materially assists in targeting further attacks against the application.

### Likelihood
Reproducible by any unauthenticated request to protected endpoints such as /api/accounts or /api/fx/rates.

### Recommendation
Disable detailed error output in production (set environment=production error handling to return only a generic error code and message). Log full stack traces server-side only. Implement a global exception handler that sanitises responses before they reach the client.

### Evidence
```
GET /api/accounts (no auth) returned HTTP 500 with: {"file":"\/var\/www\/bankofed\/src\/Services\/AuthService.php","line":77,"trace":"#0 \/var\/www\/bankofed\/src\/Middleware\/AuthMiddleware.php(28)..."}

## 72. Missing Content-Security-Policy and HSTS Headers on Admin Panel

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/admin/
- CVSS: 4.3 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
A GET request to the admin panel at http://192.168.3.101/admin/ revealed that the HTTP response omits both a Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header and a Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header. While several defensive headers are present (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection, and Referrer-Policy), the absence of CSP means the browser applies no policy restricting which scripts, stylesheets, or external resources may load. The absence of HSTS means the browser will not enforce HTTPS for subsequent visits, leaving the panel accessible over plain HTTP.

### Impact
Without CSP, any cross-site scripting vulnerability discovered on the admin panel would face no browser-level restriction on script execution or resource loading, increasing the exploitability and potential impact of such a finding. Without HSTS, an attacker in a position to intercept network traffic (e.g., on the same network segment) could downgrade the connection to HTTP and capture admin credentials or session tokens in transit.

### Likelihood
The missing headers are a configuration gap rather than a directly exploitable vulnerability. Exploitation requires either a separate XSS injection point (for CSP) or a network-level interception opportunity (for HSTS). Practical risk is therefore limited without one of these preconditions being met.

### Recommendation
1. Add a Content-Security-Policy header to the admin panel response. A restrictive starting policy would be: `default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' cdn.tailwindcss.com; style-src 'self' fonts.googleapis.com; object-src 'none'`. Note that the admin panel currently loads resources from cdn.tailwindcss.com and fonts.googleapis.com, so these origins must be included in the allowlist. Tighten the policy iteratively using CSP reporting mode before enforcing it.
2. Add a Strict-Transport-Security header: `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains`. Ensure the application is fully accessible over HTTPS before enabling HSTS to avoid locking out users.

### Evidence
```
GET /admin/ HTTP/1.1 returned HTTP 200. Observed response headers included: x-content-type-options: nosniff, x-frame-options: DENY, x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block, referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. Neither a Content-Security-Policy header nor a Strict-Transport-Security header was present in the response.
```

### Request Evidence
```
GET /admin/ HTTP/1.1
```

### Response Evidence
```
Response headers contain no Content-Security-Policy or Strict-Transport-Security headers.
```

## 73. Stored XSS via Address Book Nickname and Payee Name Fields

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A03:2021 – Injection
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/address-book
- CVSS: 5.4 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N)

### Description
The POST /api/address-book and PUT /api/address-book/:id endpoints accept and persist arbitrary HTML and JavaScript in the nickname and payee_name fields without any input sanitisation. The stored payload is returned verbatim in subsequent GET /api/address-book responses. If the banking portal renders these fields without HTML-encoding — for example, in a payee dropdown or address book list — the stored script will execute in the victim's browser.

### Impact
An authenticated attacker can store a malicious payload that executes in the browser of any user who views their address book, including the attacker's own session if shared or accessed by support staff. If the admin panel renders payee data without encoding, the payload could also execute in an administrative session, potentially enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or unauthorised actions on behalf of the victim.

### Likelihood
Exploitation requires an authenticated session to write the payload, and a separate victim to view the rendered address book in a context where the application does not HTML-encode the field. Both conditions are plausible in a multi-user banking portal. Severity is constrained by the requirement for victim interaction and the unconfirmed rendering behaviour of the front-end.

### Recommendation
Sanitise all user-supplied input at write time, rejecting or stripping HTML markup from nickname and payee_name fields — these should accept printable text only. HTML-encode all dynamic field values at render time regardless of stored content. Implement a strict Content Security Policy that disallows inline script execution. Apply the same controls to any admin-facing views that display address book data.

### Evidence
```
A POST request to /api/address-book with the nickname value set to <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> returned HTTP 201 and stored the payload verbatim. A subsequent GET /api/address-book confirmed the unmodified payload was persisted and returned in the listing response.
```

### Request Evidence
```
POST /api/address-book\nAuthorization: Bearer [user2_token]\n\n{"nickname":"<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>","payee_name":"XSS Test","payee_bsb":"062-001","payee_account_number":"12345678"}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 201: {"id":20,"nickname":"<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>","payee_name":"XSS Test",...}\nGET /api/address-book → same payload returned in listing
```

## 74. Stored XSS via Profile Update — Unsanitised HTML Persisted in Customer Name Fields

- Severity: medium
- OWASP: A03:2021 – Injection
- Source: A.L.I.C.E
- Validation: unvalidated
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/profile
- CVSS: 5.4 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N)

### Description
The PUT /api/profile endpoint accepts and persistently stores arbitrary HTML and JavaScript in user-controlled profile fields (e.g., first_name, last_name) without any sanitisation or encoding. The raw payload is returned verbatim in all subsequent GET /api/profile and login responses. Should the admin panel render these fields without HTML-encoding, the stored payload would execute as JavaScript in the administrator's browser when viewing customer records.

### Impact
If the admin panel renders customer name fields without output encoding, an authenticated attacker could execute arbitrary JavaScript in an administrator's browser session. Successful exploitation could lead to session hijacking, credential theft, or further lateral movement within the application.

### Likelihood
Exploitation requires an administrator to view the affected customer record in a rendering context that does not HTML-encode the stored value. The payload is confirmed stored and returned verbatim by the API; exploitability is therefore contingent on the admin panel's output handling, which was not fully verified during testing.

### Recommendation
1. Sanitise all user-supplied input on write: strip or reject HTML tags in profile fields that have no legitimate need for markup. 2. HTML-encode all user-controlled values at every output context (admin panel, customer portal, API responses consumed by a browser). 3. Implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) to limit inline script execution as a defence-in-depth control. 4. Apply the same controls to all profile fields, not only first_name and last_name.

### Evidence
```
A PUT /api/profile request was submitted with first_name set to "<script>alert(1)</script>". The server returned HTTP 200 and persisted the payload verbatim, as confirmed in the response body: {"first_name":"<script>alert(1)<\/script>","last_name":"XSSTest",...}. The value was present in all subsequent profile responses, confirming server-side storage without sanitisation.
```

### Request Evidence
```
PUT /api/profile\nAuthorization: Bearer [user1_token]\n\n{"first_name":"<script>alert(1)</script>","last_name":"XSSTest",...}
```

### Response Evidence
```
HTTP 200: {"first_name":"<script>alert(1)<\/script>","last_name":"XSSTest","totp_secret":"A2SZC3UPM6EUVFNFTZTKNP3EK5PYSXBZBY7SB5UBEPEJUPH5WZ6JC3RS6GYYXOZWYBEMZ46ECS5M6HLZCUEUSDVRGIFCZMIYWD4JVRQ",...}
```

## 75. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` combined with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. Additionally, `Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *` and `Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD` are set globally, including on unauthenticated endpoints.

### Impact
The combination of wildcard origin with credentials is technically invalid per the CORS specification (browsers will block it for credentialed requests), but the permissive header policy may interact unexpectedly with certain clients or proxies. The `Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *` wildcard may expose custom security headers to cross-origin inspection.

### Likelihood
Low practical browser-exploitability due to spec enforcement, but represents a misconfiguration that should be corrected.

### Recommendation
Set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins rather than `*`. Remove `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` unless cross-origin credentialed requests are genuinely required. Enumerate specific allowed headers rather than using a wildcard.

### Evidence
```
All API responses include headers: `access-control-allow-origin: *`, `access-control-allow-credentials: true`, `access-control-allow-headers: *`, `access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD`.

## 76. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true simultaneously. While browsers will not honour credentials with a wildcard origin per the CORS specification, this configuration indicates the CORS policy has not been properly designed. If the wildcard is ever replaced with arbitrary origin reflection (a common misconfiguration step), authenticated cross-origin data theft becomes immediately exploitable.

### Impact
In the current state, browsers block credentialed requests to wildcard origins, limiting direct exploitation. However, the misconfiguration signals a lack of CORS hardening and could become a high-severity issue if the origin handling is changed to reflective.

### Likelihood
Low for direct exploitation in current form due to browser enforcement. The risk is elevated by the fact that the admin panel is already unauthenticated, making CORS less relevant for those endpoints.

### Recommendation
Remove Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true from responses that use a wildcard origin. For endpoints that genuinely require credentialed cross-origin access, explicitly whitelist trusted origins rather than using a wildcard, and validate the Origin header server-side against that whitelist.

### Evidence
```
All API responses include headers: access-control-allow-origin: * and access-control-allow-credentials: true. Example observed on GET /api/fx/rates (HTTP 200) and all other API endpoints tested.

GET http://192.168.3.101/api/health

x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
access-control-allow-origin: *
access-control-allow-credentials: true
access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD
access-control-allow-headers: *
access-control-max-age: 86400
content-length: 280
keep-alive: timeout=5, max=100
connection: Keep-Alive
content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8

```

## 77. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include the headers 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true' simultaneously. Additionally, Access-Control-Allow-Headers is set to '*' and Access-Control-Allow-Methods permits all HTTP verbs. While browsers block credentialed requests to a wildcard origin, the combination signals a misconfigured CORS policy that may be trivially changed to reflect arbitrary origins.

### Impact
If the wildcard is ever changed to reflect the request Origin (a common misconfiguration step), any malicious website could make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API and read sensitive banking data on behalf of authenticated users.

### Likelihood
Currently browsers enforce the wildcard restriction, so direct exploitation is blocked. However the policy is one configuration change away from full CORS bypass. The misconfiguration is present on every API endpoint observed.

### Recommendation
Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins. Remove the wildcard. Do not combine Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with a wildcard or reflective origin policy. Restrict Access-Control-Allow-Methods and Access-Control-Allow-Headers to only what is required.

### Evidence

Every observed API response includes: 'access-control-allow-origin: *', 'access-control-allow-credentials: true', 'access-control-allow-headers: *', 'access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD'.

## 78. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *. This configuration is invalid per the CORS specification (credentials cannot be used with wildcard origin) but indicates an intent to allow broad cross-origin access. The wildcard on headers also permits any custom header.

### Impact
While browsers block credentialed requests with a wildcard origin, the configuration signals a lack of CORS hardening. If the wildcard is ever changed to reflect the requesting origin (a common misconfiguration), authenticated cross-origin requests would be possible, allowing a malicious site to call admin APIs on behalf of a logged-in admin.

### Likelihood
Low in current form due to browser enforcement, but the configuration is incorrect and represents a latent risk if the implementation changes.

### Recommendation
Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins. Remove the wildcard from Access-Control-Allow-Headers and enumerate only required headers. Ensure Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is only set when a specific trusted origin is reflected.

### Evidence
```

## 79. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. While browsers will not send cookies with a wildcard origin, the combination is a misconfiguration and indicates the CORS policy has not been carefully designed. If the origin is ever changed to reflect the requesting origin (a common misconfiguration step), it would immediately enable cross-origin credential theft.

### Impact
In the current state, browser-enforced CORS prevents credential-bearing cross-origin reads. However, the misconfigured header pair signals a policy that is one configuration change away from allowing any origin to read authenticated API responses.

### Likelihood
Low in current form due to browser enforcement of the wildcard/credentials combination. Risk escalates significantly if the server is later changed to reflect arbitrary origins.

### Recommendation
Remove Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true if a wildcard origin is intended. If credentialed cross-origin requests are required, explicitly whitelist trusted origins and validate the Origin header server-side against that whitelist.

## 81. CORS Arbitrary Origin Reflection with Credentials Enabled on Admin API

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
All API responses include 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' combined with 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true' and 'Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *'. The combination of a wildcard origin with credentials is technically invalid per the CORS specification, but the presence of both headers indicates a misconfigured policy. Additionally, the wildcard origin means any origin can make cross-origin requests.

### Impact
If the CORS policy were to reflect arbitrary origins (rather than the literal wildcard), authenticated cross-origin requests could be made from attacker-controlled sites, potentially exposing sensitive banking data. The current wildcard prevents credential sharing in compliant browsers, but the configuration is fragile and may behave differently in non-standard clients.

### Likelihood
Low in standard browsers due to the wildcard/credentials incompatibility, but the misconfiguration represents a policy failure that could be exploited if the implementation changes to origin reflection.

### Recommendation
Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins. Remove the wildcard. If credentials are required, the origin must be explicitly specified, not wildcarded. Review whether cross-origin credential sharing is actually needed.

### Evidence
```
All API responses include headers: 'access-control-allow-origin: *', 'access-control-allow-credentials: true', 'access-control-allow-headers: *', 'access-control-allow-methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, PATCH, HEAD' — observed consistently across all probed endpoints.

- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.1 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:L/A:N)

### Description
POST /api/accounts accepted a request body containing a balance field set to 999999, yet the created account was returned with balance 0.00. While the server correctly ignored the attacker-supplied balance, the endpoint accepted and processed the request without rejecting the extraneous field, indicating no strict input schema enforcement. This warrants confirmation that balance cannot be set via any other parameter combination.

### Impact
If the balance field or similar financial fields can be set via mass assignment on any account creation or update endpoint, an attacker could credit their own account with arbitrary funds.

### Likelihood
Low in this specific case — the server ignored the supplied balance value. However, the lack of strict field rejection means the attack surface should be reviewed across all financial endpoints.

### Recommendation
Implement a strict allowlist of accepted fields for all API endpoints. Explicitly reject requests containing unexpected fields, or silently ignore them with server-side allowlist enforcement. Add automated tests asserting that balance and other financial fields cannot be set via client input.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/accounts with {"balance": 999999} returned HTTP 201 with "balance":"0.00", confirming the field was silently ignored rather than rejected. No validation error was returned for the extraneous field.

## 85. User Enumeration via Distinct Login Error Message

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A07
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.7 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The /api/auth/login endpoint returns the specific error code `USER_NOT_FOUND` and message `No account found with this email address.` when an email does not exist. This allows an attacker to enumerate valid email addresses by observing the distinct response.

### Impact
An attacker can confirm which email addresses have registered accounts, enabling targeted phishing, credential stuffing, or brute-force attacks against known-valid accounts.

### Likelihood
Straightforward to automate; any attacker can enumerate the user base by iterating email addresses.

### Recommendation
Return a generic message for all failed login attempts regardless of whether the email exists (e.g., `Invalid email or password.`). Apply rate limiting and account lockout to the login endpoint.

### Evidence
```
POST /api/auth/login with `{"email":"amelia.chen@example.com","password":"password"}` returned HTTP 401 with `{"code":"USER_NOT_FOUND","message":"No account found with this email address."}`, explicitly confirming the email is not registered.

## 86. User Enumeration via Distinct Login Error Message

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A07
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.7 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The POST /api/auth/login endpoint returns the specific error code WRONG_PASSWORD when the email exists but the password is incorrect. A different error code would be expected for a non-existent email, allowing enumeration of valid accounts.

### Impact
An attacker can confirm which email addresses have registered accounts, enabling targeted phishing, credential stuffing, or brute-force attacks against confirmed accounts.

### Likelihood
Likely — the distinct WRONG_PASSWORD code is returned consistently across all six tested attempts for a known-valid email.

### Recommendation
Return a generic error message and code (e.g., INVALID_CREDENTIALS) regardless of whether the email exists or the password is wrong. Ensure timing is also normalised between both cases.

### Evidence
```
Steps 97–104: all failed logins for amelia.chen@example.com returned {"code":"WRONG_PASSWORD","message":"Incorrect password."}, distinguishing a valid email with wrong password from an unknown email.

## 87. User Enumeration via Distinct Login Error Message

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A07
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.7 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The /api/auth/login endpoint returns different error codes and messages depending on whether the submitted email address exists in the system. A non-existent email returns code 'USER_NOT_FOUND' with message 'No account found with this email address.', while a valid email with a wrong password returns code 'WRONG_PASSWORD' with message 'Incorrect password.'

### Impact
An attacker can enumerate valid user email addresses by observing the distinct error responses, enabling targeted credential stuffing or phishing attacks against confirmed accounts.

### Likelihood
Certain and trivially automated. The two distinct responses are observed in Steps 127 and 128.

### Recommendation
Return a single generic error message and error code for all failed login attempts regardless of whether the email exists, e.g. 'Invalid email or password.' Implement rate limiting and account lockout to further mitigate brute-force enumeration.

### Evidence
```
Step 127: nonexistent@example.com → 401 code 'USER_NOT_FOUND', 'No account found with this email address.' Step 128: amelia.chen@example.com (valid) → 401 code 'WRONG_PASSWORD', 'Incorrect password.'

## 88. User Input Reflected Unsanitised in API Error Message

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A03
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 2.7 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:N)

### Description
The FX convert endpoint at /api/fx/convert reflects the raw value of the 'from' query parameter directly into the error response body without sanitisation. Submitting from=AUD' returns the error message: "Exchange rate not found for currency: AUD'". While no SQL error was observed, the unfiltered reflection of special characters into error messages indicates insufficient input sanitisation.

### Impact
The immediate impact is limited as no SQL error or data leakage was observed. However, reflected content in error messages can assist attackers in probing for injection vulnerabilities and may contribute to reflected XSS if the error is ever rendered in an HTML context without encoding.

### Likelihood
Low for significant exploitation in isolation. The endpoint returns JSON and the reflection does not currently demonstrate SQL injection or XSS execution. It is noted as a hygiene issue.

### Recommendation
Validate the 'from' and 'to' currency parameters against a strict allowlist of known currency codes (e.g., 3-letter ISO 4217 codes) before use. Do not reflect raw user input in error messages; return a generic error referencing the invalid parameter name only.

### Evidence
```
GET /api/fx/convert?from=AUD'&to=USD&amount=100 returned HTTP 400 with body: {"error":{"code":"INVALID_CURRENCY","message":"Exchange rate not found for currency: AUD'"}}. The single-quote character was reflected verbatim.

## 89. Verbose Internal Error Disclosure in Admin API Responses

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.7 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
When a JWT without a `jti` claim was submitted to /api/profile, the server returned a full PHP stack trace including internal file paths (`/var/www/bankofed/src/...`), class names, method signatures, and line numbers in the JSON error response.

### Impact
Discloses the application's internal directory structure, class hierarchy, and source file layout, aiding an attacker in understanding the codebase and identifying further attack surface.

### Likelihood
Triggered by any malformed JWT; easily reproducible.

### Recommendation
Disable detailed error output in production. Catch exceptions at the middleware/router level and return only a generic error code and message. Log full stack traces server-side only.

### Evidence
```
GET /api/profile with a JWT lacking a `jti` claim returned HTTP 500 with `"message":"BankOfEd\\Services\\AuthService::isTokenRevoked()... called in /var/www/bankofed/src/Middleware/AuthMiddleware.php on line 28"` and a full `#0 ... #1 ... #2 ...` trace.

## 90. Verbose Internal Error Disclosure in Admin API Responses

- Severity: low
- OWASP: A05
- Source: Dynamic
- Validation: validating
- Affected URL: http://192.168.3.101/api/health
- CVSS: 3.5 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N)

### Description
POST /api/transfers/check with transfer_type=address_book and no address_book_id returned HTTP 500 with a full PHP stack trace, including absolute file paths (/var/www/bankofed/src/...), class names, method signatures, and line numbers.

### Impact
An attacker gains detailed knowledge of the server's directory structure, framework internals, and code flow, which can assist in targeted exploitation of other vulnerabilities.

### Likelihood
Easily triggered by any authenticated user sending a malformed but valid-looking request. No special privilege required.

### Recommendation
Disable verbose error output in production. Return a generic error message and error code to the client. Log full stack traces server-side only. Set appropriate PHP error_reporting and display_errors=Off in production configuration.

### Evidence
```
HTTP 500 response body included: "file":"/var/www/bankofed/src/Models/AddressBookEntry.php","line":18,"trace":"#0 /var/www/bankofed/src/Services/TransferService.php(27)..." exposing full server paths and class hierarchy.
